title: Orchestrated A/B-Test Anchoring with Fast-Fallback and promotion hooks
preview:
Motivation: Teams running A/B tests and canary promotions need deterministic cohort anchoring plus an automated fast-fallback when latency, error-rate, or quality regressions are detected. This PR introduces a controller and API surface that: 1) pins user/query cohorts to a stable treatment (anchor) during long-running A/B experiments, 2) provides promotion hooks to move anchored cohorts from shadow->canary->primary on a schedule or metric gate, and 3) implements a fast-fallback path that immediately drains traffic from a failing variant and re-anchors affected cohorts to the last known-good variant.

Changes include: controller implementation (orchestration/scheduler), a new lightweight cohort store (kv-backed, optional in Dedicated/Private), API surface for experiments (create/update/pause/promote), telemetry and probe instrumentation (per-cohort latency/error/quality counters), and end-to-end integration tests and docs. The description below summarizes the components and rationale.

Design highlights: - Cohort anchoring: a deterministic hashing and sticky assignment ensures the same caller/session lands in the same treatment for the experiment duration. Anchors are stored with TTL and versioned keys so rollbacks can replay previous assignments. - Promotion hooks: experiments can declare promotion policies (time-based, metric threshold, linear ramp). Promotions go through a two-phase commit: dry-run observation then switch, with a snapshot of cohort mapping. - Fast-fallback: when a policy gate trips (latency > SLO, error spike, or model-quality delta beyond threshold), the controller triggers immediate drain of failing variant and reassigns cohorts to the last healthy variant; a short-lived quarantine lane accepts replayed requests for diagnostics. - Safety and audit: all assignment changes and fallback events are journaled; operators can view the journal and roll forward/back via the console or API.

Backward compatibility: experiments default to the older routing behavior if no experiment config is present. The cohort store is optional — when disabled the controller falls back to probabilistic assignment but still enforces promotion/fallback flows.

Testing and rollout: unit and integration tests added under integrations/ab_anchor. Performance microbenchmarks show assignment latency <200us p95 and fallback completion time under 2s in a local Dedicated environment.

Why this is different from prior rollout work: prior PRs focused on traffic-splitting and staged canaries; this adds persistent cohort assignment and a fast, auditable emergency fallback designed for long-lived A/B experiments where reassigning users mid-experiment is unacceptable.

Related: Linear ENG-2345 (AB experiment lifecycle), ENG-2419 (fast-fallback SLO integration).

title: Reduce CPU serialization overhead and introduce request-affine thread pinning
preview:
Motivation: production canaries showed CPU-side stalls in the request path under medium tail-load (concurrent short requests). Root cause analysis indicated three correlated issues: a global serialization lock in the request marshalling path, KV-cache read contention on a small number of shards, and excessive cross-thread wakeups from the executor when continuous batching is active. This PR addresses those CPU-side bottlenecks with low-risk, togglable changes that reduce serialization overhead and make scheduling request-affine so hot requests avoid context migration.

Summary of changes: I) Replace global request-serialization mutex with per-worker small-buffer fast-path using FlatBuffers for marshalling and a fallback to proto when necessary. II) Introduce a request-affine thread-pool (request-affine executors) that pins a request's lifecycle to a CPU core group to reduce cache churn during multi-stage request handling. III) Add a lock-free fast read path for KV-cache using a seqlock-style version stamp per shard plus per-shard read counters, and shard rebalancer to spread hot keys. IV) Reduce continuous-batching wakeups by batching wake signals and using a wait-free work-stealing hint for low-latency tails.

Performance impact (canary + micro-bench): - End-to-end P50: -6% latency, P95: -14%, P99: -18% for short inference (<128 tokens) workloads. - CPU sys time on sandboxed 8-vCPU nodes: -35% for the runtime process when under 500rps of small requests. - Throughput improved by ~9% in multi-tenant density tests due to reduced serialization stalls.

Rollout plan: flags added to runtime config and console: runtime.cpu_optimizations.enabled (default: false), runtime.request_affinity.policy (off|pinned|affine). Default rollout: enable on Dedicated clusters for canary group A (5% traffic) for 72 hours, compare P99 and host CPU; if stable, increase to 25% then 100%. Quick rollback: set runtime.cpu_optimizations.enabled=false -> instant drain-of-new-requests (no binary change).

Compatibility and safety: changes are behind config flags. No model artifacts changed. Unit and integration tests added. This is not a breaking API change.

Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for serialization fast-path and fallback - [x] KV-cache microbench and canary harness - [x] End-to-end canary config + dashboard widgets - [x] Documentation for new runtime flags and helm values - [x] Security review (no secrets touched)

Commits: detailed below.

Notes for reviewers: focus review on correctness of the seqlock implementation (KV-cache) and on potential live-migration interactions with request affinity. I added a conservative fairness timer on the affine pool (200ms) to avoid starvation of low-priority background tasks.

Additional context: This work was driven by ENG-9123 (investigate CPU stalls) and is intended to be low-risk stepping-stone; the next PR will expose adaptive shard sizing and per-tenant affinity policies.

title: handoff: privacy filter and attachment rate-limit proxy
preview: Add a configurable PII redaction pipeline to handoff notes and an ephemeral attachment proxy with per-incident and per-user rate limits. Motivation: customers reported accidental PII leakage in handoff notes and large attachments causing spiky egress and long-term retention burdens. This PR implements a layered approach: 1) a lightweight PII classifier + heuristic regex scrubber exposed as internal/handoff/scrubber.go with three redact levels (none, redact-pii, redact-sensitive) and config driven allowlists; 2) an attachment proxy service internal/proxy/attach_proxy.go that accepts multipart uploads, applies size/type heuristics, stores content in an ephemeral blobstore with TTL and returns short access tokens; 3) enforcement of per-incident and per-user upload rate limits via a sliding-window counter stored in Redis with configurable quotas and backoff; 4) telemetry and audit hooks in slo-toolkit for redaction-rates and proxy throttling; 5) docs and runbook updates for oncall handling of throttled uploads. Backwards compatibility: the redaction pipeline is behind feature flag handoff.redaction.enabled=false by default and proxy is optional with handoff.attachments.proxy_url unset by default. Rollout plan: enable in staging for 10% of incidents for 48h, monitor redaction false-positive/false-negative rates and proxy 429 rate, then ramp to 100% if metrics are green. Includes migration to replace legacy long-lived attachment URLs with ephemeral tokens for new uploads only; existing attachments are untouched. Manual rollback: flip feature flags and scale down proxy deployment. Testing: unit tests for scrubber heuristics, integration tests for proxy upload/TTL, e2e handoff smoke tests with redaction enabled. See changed_files for implementation details and a short rollout checklist.

title: Introduce sequential probe compositor and adaptive cutover allocator
preview: Motivation: improve canary probe efficiency and reduce probe-induced noise during rollouts by composing sequential probes and allocating cutover budgets adaptively based on recent telemetry. This change introduces a Sequential Probe Compositor that stitches short-window synthetic probes into prioritized sequences to better surface phase-aligned regressions, and an Adaptive Cutover Allocator that computes per-rollout cutover budgets using traffic-conditioned stability scores and SLO-weighted credits. Key goals: lower false positives from probe collisions, preserve signal fidelity for tail latency, and enable safer gradual cutovers for large traffic shifts. Implementation notes: compositor merges probe windows across latency, throughput and error-rate signals; it maintains ordering and backpressure semantics so probes do not overload healthy replicas; allocator uses EWMA of percentile deltas and a stability-credit ledger to decide cutover percentages and cooldowns; both components expose Prometheus metrics and tracing hooks for downstream canary evaluators. Testing & rollout: added unit tests for sequence stitching and allocator math, integration harness that replays historical traffic traces to validate conservative/default budgets, and e2e traffic-driven scenarios validating fallback to existing sentinel policy. Checklist: [x] design doc referenced (ENG-2201); [x] unit + integration tests; [x] telemetry & dashboards; [x] docs/recipes update; [x] staged rollout to staging clusters. Related: ENG-2201, ENG-2283. This PR is a functional addition to perf-canary and is backwards-compatible with existing probe configs; default behavior remains unchanged until opt-in is enabled on rollout policies.

title: add kernel-hotspot tracer, kvcache offset histogram, and continuous batch pressure probe
preview: Motivation: It has been difficult to correlate kernel-level hotspots with KV cache layout and continuous-batching backpressure events in production traces. This PR introduces three complementary low-overhead probes and a small microbench harness to make cause-and-effect visible during perf investigations and automated regressions. Summary of changes: (1) kernel-hotspot-tracer: lightweight sampling probe that annotates scheduler and CUDA kernel entry/exit with a compact hotspot identifier derived from callsite and op-type. Emits NVTX ranges when available and falls back to short tracepoints for environments without NVTX. Includes FLOPS-adjusted counters to avoid over-weighting long-running kernels. (2) kvcache-offset-histogram: on-KVcache-access histogram recording offset distribution and small-window reuse counts to detect pathological stride patterns introduced by certain tokenizers or model layouts. Buckets are power-of-two with a dense region for accesses under 1KB. Exported as Prometheus-friendly counters and optional trace annotations for correlation. (3) continuous-batch-pressure-probe: emits an instantaneous batch_pressure gauge when the batch queue begins to back up and a sampled per-window pressure histogram for debug builds. Hooks into the batching controller earlier in the pipeline to capture producer-side pressure and includes a token-rate estimator to reduce noise from transient spikes. (4) simple_seq_bench microbench: executable to exercise variable-length sequences, stridey KV access patterns, and kernel-heavy ops; emits a compact trace and histogram CSV for offline analysis and dashboard ingestion. Design: probes are guarded by feature flags and sample rate knobs with defaults off in release builds. Histogram storage is ring-buffer backed and uses per-worker aggregators to minimize atomics. NVTX ranges are used when available with a fallback so traces are consistent across environments. Rollout: feature-gated behind telemetry.feature_flags.kernel_hotspot and telemetry.feature_flags.kvcache_hist, default disabled. Canary rollout plan: 5 percent traffic for one week then expand if metrics are stable. Tests: unit tests for histogram bucketing and ring buffer safety, stress test for multi-producer writes, and a short GPU integration job that runs the microbench in CI. Backwards compatibility: non-breaking and opt-in. Follow-ups: small observability ingestion parser update (observability-pack) and a future probe for kernel memory-bandwidth when cupi integration is feasible.

title: per-channel requantization + kernel fusion with KV-cache hinting to improve INT8/FP8 steady-state throughput
preview:
Title: Per-channel requantization + kernel fusion with KV-cache hinting to improve INT8/FP8 steady-state throughput

Summary / Motivation:
We observed that for real traffic profiles (small interactive requests with hot prefixes) the current INT8 execution path suffers from uneven per-head/per-channel scaling which results in lower GEMM utilization and extra dequant/quant roundtrips around attention and MLP. This PR introduces a per-channel requantization stage inside the microkernel and fuses the dequant/scale operations into the attention + MLP kernels when safe. Additionally, it adds a lightweight KV-cache hint that allows the scheduler to prefer pre-warmed KV workers when requantization is detected, reducing cold-KV stalls for hot prefixes. The goal is to increase steady-state throughput without impacting worst-case correctness or model quality.

What changed (high level):
- Introduced a per-channel requantization pass in the inference runtime that produces per-channel scale/zero offsets used by fused kernels. This avoids re-quantizing to a single global scale across wide layers.
- Fused dequant + GEMM + requant hot-paths in the GPU microkernel for attention and first MLP matmuls; fallbacks exist to existing dequant/separate-gemm path when fusion preconditions fail (e.g., unsupported layout, odd channel count).
- Added a scheduler hint (kv_cache_hint) propagated from request metadata when prefix reuse is detected; scheduler uses hint to bias placement to nodes with warm KV caches. This is advisory only and respects capacity/policy boundaries.
- FP8 compatibility: added a transparent FP8 fallback where per-channel requantization indicates numeric instability from INT8 scaling; runtime will automatically switch to FP8 kernels for that layer and emit a metric.
- Extended kernel capability negotiation so the compilation pipeline can choose fused kernels at deploy time based on model/layout characteristics.
- New microbenchmarks and regression tests + updated continuous-batching integration tests to cover fused paths.

Why this is different from prior quant work:
Previous PRs optimized global/tensor-level requantization and scheduling around KV batching. This change targets per-channel variability and moves the fusion directly into microkernels to reduce memory traffic and kernel launches. The KV hint is a low-risk way to improve locality without changing core placement policies.

Benchmarks (representative):
Environment: 1xA100-80GB, Llama-2-13B quantized to INT8 using Redwood quant pipeline, synthetic traffic replay derived from production traces (mix of short interactive and streaming requests).
- Throughput (tokens/sec): +1.65x compared to baseline INT8 path at 1k token steady-state (microbatching enabled).
- Tail latency (P95 tokens/seq, 512 context): -22% (improvement).
- Token cost estimate: -11.8% due to fewer kernel launches and better utilization.
- FP8 fallback path triggered on ~2.4% of layers in stress tests; when triggered, throughput impact limited to -8% for that request while preserving quality.

Checklist / Testing:
- [x] Unit tests for per-channel scale extraction and validation
- [x] GPU microkernel unit tests (correctness vs reference FP32 for 64 random seeds)
- [x] Integration: continuous-batching + kv-cache hint end-to-end test
- [x] Perf: added benchmark harnesses and CI perf job (used to validate +1.65x)
- [x] Model quality smoke: token-level logprob deltas across evaluation prompt-set (no regression beyond tolerances)
- [x] Safety: fallback to non-fused code and FP8 if numerical checks fail at runtime

Commits (high-level):
- a3d9f1e add per-channel scale extractor and validation logic
- b7c2d1a implement fused dequant-gemm-requant microkernel (CUDA)
- c48f0d2 extend kernel registry and capability negotiation in compile pipeline
- d90b3aa add kv_cache_hint propagation and scheduler hooks
- e1f4b55 add perf harness + benchmarks for fused path
- f0a7b23 tests: unit/integration updates and flaky test fix

Files changed (illustrative, not exhaustive):
- runtime/kernels/fused_gemm_requant.cu (new)
- runtime/quant/per_channel_scale.cc
- runtime/quant/per_channel_scale.h
- runtime/compile/kernel_registry.cc
- runtime/scheduler/kv_hint_router.cc
- runtime/scheduler/kv_hint_router_test.cc
- runtime/tests/quant_fusion_integration_test.py
- bench/perf/fused-path-bench.cc
- third_party/benchmarks/trace_replay_profiles/prod_short_prefix.json (updated trace sample)

Review conversation (high level):
- Priya Natarajan (review): "Nice work — fusion looks clean. Two asks: (1) ensure we have clear perf counters for how often FP8 fallback fires, and (2) add a guard that prevents fusion on tiny channels (channel<8)."
- Author (Ethan): "Added runtime counter `quant.fallback.fp8.count` and a channel-size guard in the kernel registry; pushed patch f0a7b23."
- Glen Ortiz (review): "Scheduler hint looks reasonable but I worry about policy escape when hints are overused. Please make hint decay configurable and add metrics for hint hit-rate."
- Author: "Added `kv_hint.decay_window_ms` config with default 60s and `kv_hint.hit_rate` metric; included in release notes."
- Marta Rios (approver): "Verified bench harness and model quality smoke locally; approved pending CI."

CI summary and flakiness notes:
- Unit: pass
- Integration: initially failed due to flaky tensor-check in microkernel test; fixed by aligning epsilon and test seeds in f0a7b23; re-ran and passed.
- Perf CI: pass on dedicated perf runners; relative performance validated against a pinned baseline commit.

Compatibility and fallback behavior:
- Fusion is opt-in per-deployment via a compile-time flag and runtime capability negotiation. Default hosted deploys will enable fusion for models that pass validation; Dedicated/Private customers can opt out via config.
- In case fusion preconditions fail or numerical checks exceed thresholds, runtime falls back to existing dequant -> GEMM -> requant path and emits metrics. FP8 fallback is automatic and logged.

Operational considerations:
- New metrics: quant.fallback.fp8.count, quant.fused.kernel.calls, quant.fused.kernel.latency_ms, kv_hint.hit_rate
- Rollout plan: canary on 5% of traffic, monitor token throughput, p95 latency, and quant.fallback.fp8.count; ramp to 25% then 100% if stable.

Related linear/Jira: ENG-2045 (per-channel quant), ENG-2122 (kv-affinity hints), INT-712 (customer report re: tail spikes)

Merge outcome:
- Merged via squash on 2026-02-20 after approvals and CI green.

Notes for follow-ups / TODOs:
- Explore automatic tuning of per-channel scale binning to reduce FP8 triggers
- Investigate kernel tiling variants for odd channel counts (small-channel fallback currently more conservative)
- Add a lightweight runtime config that exposes a "fusion aggressiveness" knob to SRE teams

If you want to reproduce perf results, see bench/perf/README.md in this branch which contains exact runner flags, model artifact hashes, and trace replay params.

title: Attenuated warm-pool smoothing and utilization exposer for dedicated fleets
preview: Context: Dedicated fleet warm pools are currently susceptible to short-lived microbursts: a handful of tenants generate transient spikes that cause the warm-pool controller to over-allocate headroom, inflate cost, and produce noisy per-tenant utilization metrics. This PR introduces an attenuated smoothing policy for warm-pool sizing and a lightweight utilization exposer that decouples instantaneous spikes from the long-running reservation logic. Motivation: reduce oscillation-driven overprovisioning, improve stability for tenant SLAs, and provide clearer telemetry to platform teams so they can tune reservations and burst contracts. Changes in this PR (high level): - Introduce an attenuated smoothing window with configurable decay factors and min/max headroom bounds. The algorithm applies exponential decay to short spikes while allowing sustained demand to grow warm pool capacity. - Add microburst detector that can tag allocations as transient (microburst) vs sustained; microburst signals are retained short-term and influence only ephemeral warm allocations. - Expose per-tenant utilization summaries (P50/P95/P99 token throughput and burst incidence rate) to telemetry pipeline with a compact protobuf payload for ingestion. - Split warm-pool decision path: "elastic" (longer smoothing) vs "ephemeral" (microburst buffer) to keep committed reservations stable. - Add feature flag and runtime knobs: warm_pool.smoothing.enabled, warm_pool.smoothing.decay_tau_ms, warm_pool.ephemeral_budget_pct, telemetry.util_bucket_window_ms. Checklist: - [x] Core algorithm and controller changes implemented - [x] Unit tests for smoothing kernel and microburst tagging - [x] Integration test that simulates mixed sustained + bursty tenants and validates headroom change bounds - [x] Telemetry schema and exporter implementation - [x] Docs: operator guide and runtime knobs - [x] Feature-flag gating for staged rollout Commits (summary): - init: add smoothing kernel and interfaces (pkg/autoscaler/smoothing.go) - controller: wire smoothing into warm-pool reconciler and split decision path (pkg/autoscaler/controller.go) - telemetry: add utilization exposer and protobuf (internal/metrics/utilization_exposer.go, proto/utilization.proto) - exporter: push compact metrics to metrics pipeline with sampling (cmd/dedicated/telemetry/exporter.go) - tests: unit and integration harness (tests/integration/warm_pool_smoothing_test.go) - docs: operational guide and examples (docs/operational/warm-pool.md) Files changed (high-level): pkg/autoscaler/smoothing.go, pkg/autoscaler/controller.go, internal/metrics/utilization_exposer.go, proto/utilization.proto, cmd/dedicated/telemetry/exporter.go, docs/operational/warm-pool.md Why this is safe: - Backwards compatible: smoothing is behind warm_pool.smoothing.enabled (default=false for initial rollout). - Telemetry schema is additive; consumers that don't parse the new proto will ignore the new field. - Behavior can be reverted by toggling feature flag or adjusting decay_tau_ms to a neutral value. Testing notes: - Local integration harness simulates 100 tenants with 10% producing 2s microbursts and confirms <7% warm-pool capacity change compared to baseline. - Unit tests cover edge cases including zero traffic, sustained ramp, and pathological bursts. Review conversation summary: - Ethan: requested clearer bounds on min/max headroom and unit for decay_tau_ms. I added validation and docs explaining units are milliseconds and provided reasonable defaults (decay_tau_ms=5000ms, min_headroom=1, max_headroom=5). - Miguel: asked for an additional metric to report number of microburst-tagged allocations per minute; added microburst_alloc_count and unit test. - Ava: suggested moving the telemetry exporter to a separate goroutine to avoid blocking reconciler; refactored exporter to use buffered channel and background worker and added backpressure handling. CI / checks: - CI run 1: go vet + unit tests (flaky DB connection in test harness) -> failed; reran. - CI run 2: full unit + integration (short) -> pass. Observability: - dashboards: added warm-pool smoothing dashboard panel (docs/operational/warm-pool.md) showing headroom, microburst rate, and utilization percentiles. - Alerts: recommend adding alert warm_pool.unexpected_growth if headroom increases >50% over 5m window. Migration/rollout plan: - Stage 1 (dev): enable smoothing for dev clusters for 48h; monitor microburst_alloc_count and headroom trend. - Stage 2 (staging): enable for 1 week, bounded by feature-flag autoswitch if warm_pool.unexpected_growth fires. - Stage 3 (canary->prod): gradual ramp to 10% fleets, then 30%, with automated rollback criteria. Merge outcome: Squash-merged into main after approvals and green CI. Merge commit: abc123def4567890. Release notes: Added attenuated warm-pool smoothing to reduce reactive overprovisioning from short-lived tenant microbursts; introduced per-tenant utilization exposer (P50/P95/P99 + microburst incidence); feature flag warm_pool.smoothing.enabled controls rollout. Followups / TODOs: - ENG-6012: surface per-tenant utilization in Console route. - ENG-5687: tune default decay_tau_ms via A/B tests across dev fleets.

title: Atomicize SSE chunk handshake and idempotent replay to fix backpressure edgecases
preview:
Context: Customers reported rare production hangs when streaming long responses with function calls and structured JSON boundaries via the OpenAI-compat endpoint. Symptoms included stalled client streams when upstream frames were coalesced across network segments, duplicated trailing delimiters on reconnect, and occasional corrupt partial-JSON emitted to downstream consumers. Repro: long chat with streaming function_call followed by large JSON output and intermittent packet drops; reproduce with tests/sse/test_fragment_replay.py and a patched netem dropping 1/1000 packets. Root cause: two interacting issues: (1) the handshake/ack window between API gateway and runtime allowed non-atomic flushes where a delimiter byte could be emitted without its subsequent JSON tail when the downstream backpressure closed mid-chunk; (2) replay logic was non-idempotent for partially-applied fragments, causing duplicated delimiters and out-of-order function-call sentinel emission on reconnect. Changes: 1) Introduce an atomic chunk handshake: a 1-byte preamble (0x7E) announces an incoming logical frame and the runtime now buffers until frame-complete or explicit EOT; handshake moves boundary decisions to sender to prevent orphaned delimiter emission. 2) Add idempotent replay tokens at the fragment granularity: every fragment now carries a stable fragment-id and sequence checksum so replays can be deduped safely. 3) Tighten delimiter handling: normalize custom delimiter to 

 and ensure trimming only at frame assembly stage to avoid UTF-8 split issues. 4) Prevent function_call split: implement a sentinel reassembler that guarantees function_call start/end tokens are not split across coalesced network reads. 5) Backpressure windows: gate flushes on ACK from the gateway and introduce a soft timeout fallback to avoid head-of-line blocking under partial failure. 6) Add extensive unit + integration tests and a synthetic long-stream scenario in tests/bench/stream_replay_bench.py. Rollout: feature-flagged behind runtime.feature.sse_atomic_handshake; staged Canary rollout to Dedicated clusters for 72h, then broad rollout. Backout: flip feature flag; revert is a single commit removal. Risk/compatibility: no API-level breaking changes; new preamble is internal within runtime transport and only enabled via feature flag; release_notes included below. Testing: added ~60 unit tests, 5 integration tests, and a regression harness that reproduces previous failures. See commits for details and ci checks below.

title: Introduce invocation affinity matrix and proactive alert resolver
preview:
Motivation: We observe recurring noisy SLO alerts created by correlated bursts that cross tenant thresholds without reflecting distinct root causes. Current per-invocation alerts fire independently and cause paging fatigue. This PR adds a first-class invocation affinity metric and a proactive alert resolver that groups related invocations into cohorts and suppresses/attenuates alerts when an upstream affinity signal indicates a single shared cause.

What this PR does: Adds a streaming affinity index computed at aggregation time (invocation_affinity{route, model, tenant, window}) derived from co-occurrence of invocation fingerprints, kernel contention signals, and token-phase overlap. Adds a Grafana panel to visualize the affinity matrix and an alert resolver that consumes the affinity index to decide alert coalescing and attenuation thresholds. The resolver is opt-in and governed by per-route policies (console + config).

Design notes: The affinity index is an approximate, bounded sketch (count-min + LRU eviction) to limit cardinality. Affinity score = weighted sum( fingerprint_cooccurrence, kernel_backpressure_correlation, kv_cache_overlap, token_phase_alignment ). We expose both raw and normalized scores to aid dashboarding and tuning. The proactive resolver applies a configurable hysteresis window and supports three actions: suppress, attenuate (raise threshold), and group (single alert with multi-invocation details).

Telemetry/metrics added: - invocation_affinity_score (histogram) - invocation_affinity_cohort_size (gauge) - resolver_action_count (counter) - resolver_decision_latency_ms (summary) - cohort_correlation_errors (counter)

Dashboards: New panel under Observability / Invocation cohorts: affinity matrix heatmap, cohort size timeseries, resolver action rate sparkline, example invocation bundle drilldown. Panel includes quick-links to related traces and log samples with pre-populated filters.

Alerting: Introduces an experimental alert rule template that leverages the resolver to reduce duplicate alerts across highly-affined invocations. Default behavior is off; rollout is gated behind a feature flag and gradual per-tenant opt-in.

Rollout and safety: - Feature gated by feature_flag:observability.invocation_affinity - Default config: resolver off for free-tier tenants; gradual enable for enterprise tenants with 2-week canary. - Fallback: resolver is stateless; if it fails, rules fall back to existing per-invocation alerts. All resolver decisions are logged to an audit metric for post-rollout analysis.

Testing: Unit tests for affinity sketch behavior, e2e tests that simulate correlated bursts, integration tests for dashboard links. Added a synthetic replay job that replays historical bursts to validate resolver attenuation without impacting production.

Performance: The affinity sketch uses sharded in-memory buckets with a soft size cap; measured median extra CPU per serving shard ~0.8%, 95p ~3.1% under heavy burst workloads. Token latency overhead measured <0.5ms tail in internal bench runs.

Security and privacy: Affinity calculations are performed on hashed invocation fingerprints; no raw prompt content is persisted. Access to cohort drilldown requires the existing tracing/dashboard RBAC scope.

Migration: No DB migrations. Config schema adds optional resolver policies in existing observability config.

Next steps: - Monitor resolver_action_count and cohort_correlation_errors for the first canary tenants - Iterate on dashboard UX based on SE feedback - Add an operator CLI to bulk-opt-in tenants once safe.

title: add incident-capture CLI plus ephemeral playbook templates and safe artifact bundler
preview:
Motivation: oncall responders repeatedly need a compact, reproducible snapshot of system state that is safe to share with SRE/eng. Existing runbook generators either produce too much PII or require interactive console steps. This PR introduces a lightweight CLI that: 1) collects a tunable set of diagnostics (logs, system metrics, trace snippets, recent error samples), 2) performs automated redaction rules and entropy checks to avoid shipping secrets, and 3) packages artifacts into an ephemeral bundle with a reproducible hash and a short-lived presigned link for sharing. In addition, we include a small library of ephemeral playbook templates (network-isolation, safe-restart, rolling-back) that are parameterized and renderable by the incident-bot UI.

What changed (high level): added new binary `cmd/incident-capture` with subcommands `collect`, `redact`, `bundle`, `preview`; new Go package `pkg/capture` that encapsulates collectors and redactors; playbook templates under `playbooks/ephemeral` with YAML templates and parameter schema; a lightweight HTML previewer for the bundle summary in `ui/components/BundledSummary`; tests for redaction heuristics; added guard rails that refuse to include full heap dumps unless the caller passes `--include-heap-dump` with an acknowledged flag; added telemetry events (opt-in) and a CLI dry-run mode.

Checklist: - [x] design doc linked in Linear (ENG-2345) - [x] unit tests for redaction rules - [x] e2e smoke collection in CI - [x] safety review by security team - [x] UX review for playbook templates

Backward compatibility & migration: no changes to the incident-bot API surface. New CLI is additive. Existing runbooks continue to work. Operators can enable the presigned link feature via a new feature flag `ephemeralBundles.enabled` (defaults to false in enterprise/private installs).

title: introduce-lease-prefetch-and-adaptive-coalesce-to-reduce-streaming-stalls
preview:
Motivation: Production traces show intermittent stalls (~30-120ms) at the streaming emission boundary during mixed small/large request workloads. Root cause analysis pointed to tight backpressure interactions between continuous microbatching and KV-cache cold walks: microbatches would wait for a minimal credit window while concurrent large requests evicted warm KV pages, causing emission stalls when the next token attempt triggered a cache walk and kernel rebind. This PR introduces a lease-driven prefetch and an adaptive coalesce window in the emitter to smooth those interactions and reduce TTFB variance.

What this change does: 1) Lease-based KV prefetch: when a request starts streaming, it obtains a short-lived soft lease for recent KV ranges used in the request's prefix. The lease prevents immediate eviction and primes a background prefetch worker to warm KV pages asynchronously. Leases are low-overhead and expire quickly to avoid long-term memory pressure. 2) Adaptive coalesce window: instead of a static microloop flush cadence, the emitter now adjusts its coalesce duration based on recent emission latency and current credit headroom. The window expands briefly under noisy cache conditions to allow more cooperative microbatching and contracts when latency targets are met. 3) Scheduler hook and lease-aware kernel handoff: the kernel scheduler respects active leases during kernel placement to avoid moving model shards that would trigger a remote cache miss. 4) Metrics and feature flags: added new metrics (stream.lease.hits, stream.lease.evictions, stream.coalesce.window_ms) and runtime flags runtime.streaming.lease_prefetch and runtime.streaming.adaptive_coalesce.

Design notes: - Leases are soft: they bias eviction but do not block urgent reclaims when memory pressure is high. Eviction honor rules were extended to include a grace_count which allows a lease to survive a small number of eviction cycles. - Prefetch is backgrounded to the I/O worker pool with priority gating so it cannot starve inference threads. Prefetch is conservative: we limit pages per-second and use a token-bucket per-GPU to bound bandwidth. - Adaptive coalesce uses a PID-inspired controller (proportional + integral) with an exception for sudden drops in credit (hedge) to avoid growing windows when throughput suffers. Tunables default to conservative values validated in the lab.

Files changed (high level): - runtime/emitter/lease_prefetch.{cpp,h}: lease lifecycle, eviction hooks, prefetch scheduler. - runtime/emitter/coalesce_controller.{cpp,h}: adaptive coalesce logic and controller. - runtime/scheduler/kernel_placement.cpp: respect leases during placement decisions. - runtime/metrics/streaming_metrics.cc: new stream.* metrics and exporter hooks. - tests/streaming/lease_prefetch_tests.cc: unit and integration tests that simulate eviction storms and mixed workloads. - bench/bench_stream_mixed.py: benchmark harness updates. - docs/runtime/streaming.md: operational notes and flags.

Commits summary: - 1f9b2c4: add lease data structure and basic lifecycle - 2a7d1e8: implement background prefetcher and token-bucket gating - 7c4d9aa: adaptive coalesce controller + controller tuning harness - a3b6ef1: extend eviction policy with grace_count and lease awareness - d9f4b20: add metrics and diagnostic traces - ba1e40c: tests and benchmark updates - cf53d11: docs and flags

Benchmarks: All numbers are median of 5 runs on a 4xA100 testbed with mixed workload (70% short chat streams, 30% long completions) at 250 concurrent streams. Baseline (main at 2026-01-28): median TTFB 42ms, p95 TTFB 210ms, p99 410ms. With lease-prefetch + adaptive coalesce: median TTFB 34ms (19% down), p95 TTFB 140ms (33% down), p99 260ms (36% down). Tests and validation: - Unit tests for lease lifecycle, eviction grace, and controller edge cases. - Integration test that recreates eviction storm and asserts no emission stalls > 200ms in 95% of cases. - ADE tests ran in CI (no regression). - Canary: staged rollout to 10% of hosts for 48 hours; internal telemetry confirmed p95 improvements and no increased OOMs.

Review conversation (high level): - Liam: asked for clarity on eviction semantics and whether leases can hide memory leaks. Response: leases are time-limited and the grace_count only allows transient survival; we added telemetry to track long-lived leases and fail-safe to disable prefetch if leak symptoms are detected. - Priya: requested tuning knobs exposed and a migration path for Dedicated customers. Response: all flags are exposed in runtime flags and we documented recommended values and a gated rollout for Dedicated (commit included). - Diego: flagged a race in kernel_placement; fixed in commit a3b6ef1 and added regression test. Approvals: Liam and Priya approved after the fixes; Diego gave LGTM on second pass.

CI: Initial CI run had one flaky test in bench harness (timing sensitive) — rerun fixed it. Full CI green (unit, integration, lint).

Main risk is additional transient memory use and prefetch bandwidth; mitigations: token-bucket, soft leases, and runtime kill-switch.

text:
Motivation: Production traces show intermittent stalls (~30-120ms) at the streaming emission boundary during mixed small/large request workloads. Root cause analysis pointed to tight backpressure interactions between continuous microbatching and KV-cache cold walks: microbatches would wait for a minimal credit window while concurrent large requests evicted warm KV pages, causing emission stalls when the next token attempt triggered a cache walk and kernel rebind. This PR introduces a lease-driven prefetch and an adaptive coalesce window in the emitter to smooth those interactions and reduce TTFB variance.

What this change does: 1) Lease-based KV prefetch: when a request starts streaming, it obtains a short-lived soft lease for recent KV ranges used in the request's prefix. The lease prevents immediate eviction and primes a background prefetch worker to warm KV pages asynchronously. Leases are low-overhead and expire quickly to avoid long-term memory pressure. 2) Adaptive coalesce window: instead of a static microloop flush cadence, the emitter now adjusts its coalesce duration based on recent emission latency and current credit headroom. The window expands briefly under noisy cache conditions to allow more cooperative microbatching and contracts when latency targets are met. 3) Scheduler hook and lease-aware kernel handoff: the kernel scheduler respects active leases during kernel placement to avoid moving model shards that would trigger a remote cache miss. 4) Metrics and feature flags: added new metrics (stream.lease.hits, stream.lease.evictions, stream.coalesce.window_ms) and runtime flags runtime.streaming.lease_prefetch and runtime.streaming.adaptive_coalesce.

Design notes: - Leases are soft: they bias eviction but do not block urgent reclaims when memory pressure is high. Eviction honor rules were extended to include a grace_count which allows a lease to survive a small number of eviction cycles. - Prefetch is backgrounded to the I/O worker pool with priority gating so it cannot starve inference threads. Prefetch is conservative: we limit pages per-second and use a token-bucket per-GPU to bound bandwidth. - Adaptive coalesce uses a PID-inspired controller (proportional + integral) with an exception for sudden drops in credit (hedge) to avoid growing windows when throughput suffers. Tunables default to conservative values validated in the lab.

Files changed (high level): - runtime/emitter/lease_prefetch.{cpp,h}: lease lifecycle, eviction hooks, prefetch scheduler. - runtime/emitter/coalesce_controller.{cpp,h}: adaptive coalesce logic and controller. - runtime/scheduler/kernel_placement.cpp: respect leases during placement decisions. - runtime/metrics/streaming_metrics.cc: new stream.* metrics and exporter hooks. - tests/streaming/lease_prefetch_tests.cc: unit and integration tests that simulate eviction storms and mixed workloads. - bench/bench_stream_mixed.py: benchmark harness updates. - docs/runtime/streaming.md: operational notes and flags.

title: Hierarchy-aware traffic anchoring for deferred canaries with phase-aware rollback oracle
preview: Motivation: Large multi-service deployments require staged canaries that respect logical hierarchy and shared resources. This PR introduces a hierarchy-aware traffic anchoring controller that attaches canary phases to a stability anchor within a service hierarchy and uses phase-aware rollback heuristics to reduce noisy rollbacks across sibling cohorts. Key design: 1) Anchor graph model representing parent-child service relationships and resource affinity to compute safe traffic slots for deferred canaries 2) Phase windows with per-phase safety budgets and decaying anomaly thresholds 3) Rollback-oracle that considers sibling stability, global SLO burn rate, and momentum of metrics to make automated rollback decisions 4) Graceful handoff: anchor holds traffic while downstream verifies session-consistent swaps and drain hooks. Backwards compatibility: default rollout behavior unchanged unless rollout.strategy.anchor_hierarchy is enabled. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for anchor graph builder and allocator - [x] Integration tests for phased canary flow and session-affinity during swap - [x] E2E in perf-canary lab with synthetic load - [x] Console UI flags and rollout YAML schema bump - [x] Telemetry hooks for anchor and phase events Changes include lightweight controller, allocator, and operator-level telemetry plus YAML schema updates. Commits summary: commit 1: initial anchor graph and allocator implementation commit 2: phase window and safety budget logic commit 3: rollback-oracle prototype and tests commit 4: telemetry, metrics, and console flag wiring commit 5: docs and examples Updated files: controllers/anchor_controller.go controllers/allocator/graph.go controllers/rollback_oracle.go apis/rollout/v1alpha1/anchor_types.go docs/rollouts/anchor_hierarchy.md examples/rollouts/hierarchy-anchored-canary.yaml console/src/components/RolloutFlags.ts Files changed include controller logic, small schema addition, telemetry counters, and example manifests. Testing/CI: initial CI run showed flaky integ test (timeout) that was addressed by increasing test timeout and adding a deterministic seed; final CI status is pass with full unit, integration and lint. Performance: allocation adds negligible CPU (<2% at 2000 rps) and latency impact limited to controller reconciliation cycles; allocator caches computed slots for 60s and supports batched reconciliation to amortize work. Observability: new metrics redwood_rollout_anchor_allocations_total redwood_rollout_phase_transitions_total redwood_rollout_rollback_decisions{reason} and tracing spans for AnchorAllocate and RollbackDecision. Rollback behavior: oracle evaluates windowed deltas for latency/error/quality signals, computes sibling-aware severity, and will trigger staged rollback with holdpoints if sibling instability detected. Operational notes: - The feature is behind a feature flag rollout.strategy.anchor_hierarchy; enable per-namespace via annotation rollout.redwood.io/anchor-hierarchy=enabled - Default safety budgets are conservative and can be tuned via rollout.spec.anchor.safetyBudget - This controller requires no infra changes but recommends adding anchor graphs for services with shared dependencies Migration plan: gradual opt-in via examples/rollouts/hierarchy-anchored-canary.yaml followed by monitoring of anchor allocation metrics. Related work: builds on prior staged canary controllers and cohort-synchronous splitters but focuses on hierarchy constraints to avoid cascading rollbacks. Linear tickets: ENG-5982 (anchor controller) ENG-6013 (rollback oracle).

title: Introduce kernel-level heatmap and cost-flow annotator for invocation traces
preview: Motivation: We need a low-overhead way to surface GPU/kernel-level queuing and contention hotspots correlated to token-level cost and per-stage latency. This PR adds a sampled, lightweight kernel-heatmap export and a cost-flow annotator that stitches stage timing with kernel occupancy to help SREs and runtime engineers diagnose expensive invocation patterns. Design summary: 1) A new kernel heatmap sampler emits compact histograms of kernel queue wait and execution time per-device and per-invocation-phase; sampling is prioritized for requests that exceed a configurable latency or cost threshold. 2) A cost-flow annotator postprocesses traces to attach a per-invocation-stage cost attribution vector (model load, key-value cache miss penalties, kernel stalls, token-generation time) and synthesizes rollup markers used by dashboards. 3) Minimal on-path overhead: sampler uses atomic ring-buffers and amortized aggregation at a local telemetry worker; annotation is done asynchronously by the trace aggregator to avoid adding tail latency. Implementation details: Added a new runtime probe (runtime/probes/kernel_sampler.cc) with export hooks to the observability pipeline, a proto schema (telemetry/protos/kernel_heatmap.proto) and a canonical generator for the annotator (observability/annotators/cost_flow_annotator.go). Also updated the console dashboards (dashboards/infra/kernel-heatmap-panel.json and dashboards/infra/cost-flow-explorer.json) and the SLO playbook (runbooks/kernel-contention.md). Rollout plan: feature gated behind observability.kernel_heatmap flag, disabled by default for existing customers. Canary enabled for internal clusters and a stage behind the rollout gate; follow-on step will enable 0.1% sampling for hosted tier and 1% for dedicated, then iterate. Backfill: included a small backfill job (observability/scripts/backfill_heatmap.py) to index recent traces for correlation testing. Testing: unit tests for sampler ring-buffer and proto serialization, integration test that validates annotations appear on traces end-to-end, and a perf microbenchmark showing <1.3% median p99 increase when sampling at 0.1% on benchmark lab. Migration/rollback: feature flag removal and a short migration script to drop the temporary heatmap index if disabled. Related: ENG-5421 (investigation into GPU contention during long prompts), ties to runbook playbooks introduced in previous telemetry work.

title: Queue-horizon throttling and KV probe backoff for continuous batcher
preview: Motivation: recent production incidents showed pathological tail latency when short-lived high-priority traffic interleaved with KV-heavy long-context requests. The continuous batcher could become congested by probe/burst requests that pin KV slots and prevent fairness tokens from progressing, causing latency spikes. This PR introduces a queue-horizon throttling policy and an exponential backoff for KV probe requests to reduce transient pin contention while preserving throughput.

title: Proportional backfill allocator and soft-reservation smoothing for Dedicated fleets
preview:
Motivation: Dedicated fleets often have fragmented reserved capacity where tenants underutilize their reservations for short windows. Current behavior returns unused tokens aggressively which leads to oscillation when reservations are re-acquired and contributes to noisy autoscaler decisions. This PR implements a proportional backfill allocator plus soft-reservation smoothing to allow unused reserved capacity to be temporarily shared across tenants in a controlled, decaying manner.

What this changes (single string): 1) Proportional backfill allocator: when there is spare reserved headroom, we compute per-tenant proportional backfill based on their active demand and reservation weight. Backfill amounts are distributed as fractional credits with deterministic rounding to avoid starvation. Backfill is tagged with ttl and a reclaimable flag so original owners can reclaim capacity within the reclaim window. 2) Soft-reservation smoothing: when reservations change (scale down/release), we do not return all freed capacity immediately. Instead we put freed tokens into a smoothing pool that decays with configurable half-life (default: 60s). This reduces allocation volatility when tenants frequently adjust reservations or experience microbursts. 3) Autoscaler policy hook: autoscaler now includes a backfill-aware decision step. Scale-down decisions subtract smoothing pool before computing headroom so we avoid premature scale-ups triggered by transient backfill. 4) Telemetry: new metrics added - fleet.backfill.utilization_pct, fleet.backfill.reclaimed_events_total, fleet.backfill.decay_pool_size, tenant.backfill.received_tokens_total. Dashboards: new console dashboard for backfill behavior and a backfill heatmap for the last 24h. 5) Feature flag: opt-in behind config proportional_backfill_v1; default disabled for existing fleets. 6) Tests: unit + integration tests that simulate reservation churn, reclaim races, and fairness checks.

Checklist: - [x] Core allocator + smoothing implementation - [x] Unit tests (edge rounding/TTL) - [x] Integration test covering reclaim races - [x] Metrics emitted and dashboard wiring - [x] Docs for ops and rollout - [x] Feature flag gating and safe defaults - [x] Manual canary run in staging (see rollout notes)

Files changed (high level): allocator core, smoothing state machine, autoscaler hook, metrics registry, console dashboard, docs, feature flag config, tests and CI pipeline additions for integration tests. No public API changes to the Redwood API surface.

Review conversation (summary):
- Liam: raised concern about fairness under extreme skew (90/10) and suggested a cap per-tenant to avoid monopolization. PR added a per-tenant max_backfill_pct and tests.
- Priya: asked for explicit reclaim semantics when original owner scales back up rapidly; I added a reclaim window (default 15s) and deterministic priority for reclaiming older backfill credits first.
- Evan: requested telemetry labels for fleet_id and reservation_type; added those labels and lightweight cardinality guards.
- QA: flagged a timing flake in integration test; fixed by moving from wall-clock sleeps to a simulated tickable clock in tests.
- Security: validated no elevation of privilege since backfill is an allocator layer only and does not change auth.

CI notes: new integration tests increase pipeline time by ~2m. First run had a flaky that was fixed; final CI status is pass.

Rollout plan: gate behind proportional_backfill_v1 feature flag. Start with canary fleets (staging and two low-traffic prod fleets) for 48h, observe fleet.backfill.* metrics and autoscaler scale-up rates. If stable, expand to 20% of dedicated fleets for 7 days, then full rollout. Emergency rollback: flip feature flag off.

Backwards compatibility / risk: opt-in behavior by default. The allocator adds an internal pool but does not change reservation accounting semantics. Risk mode: incorrect smoothing params could increase tail latency for under-reserved tenants; mitigated by conservative default params and telemetry.

Release notes: Introduces a controlled proportional backfill allocator and soft-reservation smoothing for Dedicated fleets (feature-flagged). Adds telemetry and a console dashboard. Opt-in default.

title: Hardening: entitlement sync job retries, idempotency keys, and SRE alert hooks
preview:
## Context
We’ve had intermittent marketplace onboarding/provisioning delays where a customer subscribes in AWS/GCP Marketplace but Redwood does not reflect entitlement quickly enough (or at all) due to transient upstream errors and non-idempotent reprocessing. This has shown up as:
- SUP-4821: entitlement/provisioning delay after subscription (support escalation)
- SUP-4829: confusion from partial sync + billing dimension mismatch during replays

Root cause in recent cases: the entitlement sync worker treats some partner/API failures as terminal, and on retry/replay can double-emit "entitlement_granted" side effects (email/webhook + provisioning steps), leading to noisy logs and occasional inconsistent state.

## What’s in this PR
1) **Retries + backoff for upstream calls**
- Adds bounded exponential backoff with jitter for marketplace provider fetches (AWS entitlement lookup + GCP entitlement listing).
- Retries only on retryable classes (timeouts, 429, 5xx, connection resets). Does *not* retry on 4xx validation errors.
- Enforces a max wall-clock budget per job run to avoid thundering herds.

2) **Idempotency keys for entitlement processing**
- Introduces an idempotency key per (provider, account, subscription_id, entitlement_version) for:
  - entitlement upsert
  - event emission (provisioning + audit/event bus)
- Ensures that reruns (manual or scheduled) do not create duplicate side effects.
- Adds a DB uniqueness constraint and a best-effort "already processed" fast-path.

3) **SRE hooks: metrics + alerting signals**
- Emits explicit metrics for:
  - `marketplace_entitlement_sync_attempts_total{provider, outcome}`
  - `marketplace_entitlement_sync_duration_ms{provider}`
  - `marketplace_entitlement_sync_retryable_failures_total{provider}`
  - `marketplace_entitlement_sync_backlog_depth`
- Adds alert conditions (wired to existing alert manager routing):
  - sustained retryable failures > threshold (10m)
  - p95 sync duration regression (30m)
  - backlog depth not draining (15m)
- Includes runbook link + dashboard panel IDs in the alert annotations.

4) **Safer job semantics**
- Makes sync job processing idempotent across restarts by persisting a per-run `sync_run_id` and using it only for tracing (not for state transitions).
- Adds explicit "partial failure" outcome codes so we can distinguish: upstream failures vs internal DB deadlocks vs provider throttling.

## How to test
- Unit tests:
  - retry classification matrix (timeouts/429/5xx vs 4xx)
  - idempotency key generation stability
  - event emission dedupe
- Integration tests (uses the existing marketplace stub server):
  - simulate 2x transient 503 then 200; verify eventual success with single entitlement record + single event
  - simulate provider throttling (429); verify backoff + job budget
  - replay same subscription sync; verify no duplicate side effects

## Operational notes
- Default retry policy: max 6 attempts, base delay 250ms, max delay 5s, full jitter.
- A single run is capped at 45s wall-clock to prevent overlap with the next scheduled invocation.
- This should reduce time-to-entitlement for marketplace customers and prevent duplicate provisioning steps when support triggers manual reruns.

## Follow-ups
- (ENG-22114) add funnel instrumentation to correlate entitlement sync latency with onboarding drop-off.
- Move alert thresholds to config once we have 2 weeks of baseline after launch.

CC: @Cole Summers for billing/metering semantics; @Nadia Rahman for metrics naming consistency.

Root cause in recent cases: the entitlement sync worker treats some partner/API failures as terminal, and on retry/replay can double-emit "entitlement_granted" side effects (email/webhook + provisioning steps), leading to noisy logs and occasional inconsistent state.

## What’s in this PR
1) **Retries + backoff for upstream calls**
- Adds bounded exponential backoff with jitter for marketplace provider fetches (AWS entitlement lookup + GCP entitlement listing).
- Retries only on retryable classes (timeouts, 429, 5xx, connection resets). Does *not* retry on 4xx validation errors.
- Enforces a max wall-clock budget per job run to avoid thundering herds.

2) **Idempotency keys for entitlement processing**
- Introduces an idempotency key per (provider, account, subscription_id, entitlement_version) for:
  - entitlement upsert
  - event emission (provisioning + audit/event bus)
- Ensures that reruns (manual or scheduled) do not create duplicate side effects.
- Adds a DB uniqueness constraint and a best-effort "already processed" fast-path.

3) **SRE hooks: metrics + alerting signals**
- Emits explicit metrics for:
  - `marketplace_entitlement_sync_attempts_total{provider, outcome}`
  - `marketplace_entitlement_sync_duration_ms{provider}`
  - `marketplace_entitlement_sync_retryable_failures_total{provider}`
  - `marketplace_entitlement_sync_backlog_depth`
- Adds alert conditions (wired to existing alert manager routing):
  - sustained retryable failures > threshold (10m)
  - p95 sync duration regression (30m)
  - backlog depth not draining (15m)
- Includes runbook link + dashboard panel IDs in the alert annotations.

4) **Safer job semantics**
- Makes sync job processing idempotent across restarts by persisting a per-run `sync_run_id` and using it only for tracing (not for state transitions).
- Adds explicit "partial failure" outcome codes so we can distinguish: upstream failures vs internal DB deadlocks vs provider throttling.

## How to test
- Unit tests:
  - retry classification matrix (timeouts/429/5xx vs 4xx)
  - idempotency key generation stability
  - event emission dedupe
- Integration tests (uses the existing marketplace stub server):
  - simulate 2x transient 503 then 200; verify eventual success with single entitlement record + single event
  - simulate provider throttling (429); verify backoff + job budget
  - replay same subscription sync; verify no duplicate side effects

## Operational notes
- Default retry policy: max 6 attempts, base delay 250ms, max delay 5s, full jitter.
- A single run is capped at 45s wall-clock to prevent overlap with the next scheduled invocation.
- This should reduce time-to-entitlement for marketplace customers and prevent duplicate provisioning steps when support triggers manual reruns.

## Follow-ups
- (ENG-22114) add funnel instrumentation to correlate entitlement sync latency with onboarding drop-off.
- Move alert thresholds to config once we have 2 weeks of baseline after launch.

CC: @Cole Summers for billing/metering semantics; @Nadia Rahman for metrics naming consistency.

title: Add marketplace SKU IDs to billing catalog + map to metering dimensions (v2)
preview:
## Context
We’re refreshing AWS + GCP marketplace listings and moving to a new SKU catalog (Hosted/Dedicated/Private + add-ons). To support clean procurement paths and reduce onboarding/billing friction, the core billing catalog needs stable marketplace-facing SKU identifiers and an explicit mapping from marketplace dimensions -> internal meters.

This PR adds first-class `marketplace_sku_id` entries to the billing catalog and defines the authoritative mapping to internal meter keys used by the usage pipeline.

## What changed
- Adds `marketplace_sku_id` to billing catalog entries for all marketplace-eligible products (Hosted, Dedicated, Private, and add-ons).
- Introduces a `marketplace_dimension_mapping` table that maps:
  - provider (`aws` | `gcp`)
  - marketplace dimension name/id
  - Redwood `marketplace_sku_id`
  - internal meter key (what we actually record against)
- Adds validation to prevent:
  - duplicate `marketplace_sku_id`
  - dimension mapping referencing unknown catalog items
  - dimension mapping referencing non-metered catalog items
  - mismatched provider constraints (AWS uses dimension strings; GCP uses SKU/billing dimension pairs)
- Ensures backwards compatibility for existing direct-billed plans: no behavior change unless `billing_source == marketplace`.

## Why
- Avoids the class of issues where marketplace dimension names drift from internal meter keys (see SUP-4829).
- Makes metering reconciliation deterministic for Finance/RevRec (one place to audit).
- Unblocks docs + onboarding work that needs stable identifiers for “what did you buy?” and “what limits apply?”

## Rollout / operational notes
1) Deploy to staging with marketplace sandbox subscriptions enabled.
2) Validate end-to-end metering in staging using the entitlement verification endpoints (ENG-22110/PR-19875) and confirm the expected dimensions hit the correct internal meters.
3) Deploy to prod behind existing marketplace gating (no new customer impact unless subscription is marketplace).
4) Post-deploy: run the reconciliation job for 24h and confirm there are no unknown/uncategorized marketplace dimensions.

## Testing
- Unit tests for catalog schema validation and mapping validation.
- Integration test that loads the full catalog and asserts all marketplace SKUs have an internal meter mapping.

## Notes for reviewers
- Naming follows the guidance from Partnerships: stable, provider-agnostic IDs in the catalog, provider-specific mapping tables.
- If we need to rename any `marketplace_sku_id`, please call it out now—these IDs are meant to be durable across listing copy iterations.

Refs:
- Linear: ENG-22111, PM-1844
- Support: SUP-4829
- Related PRs: PR-19875 (entitlement verification), PR-19877 (telemetry events)

title: Add tiered batching profile schema (fast/standard/cost-efficient) with validation + backcompat
preview: Summary: This PR introduces the config schema and validation needed to ship tiered batching defaults (fast / standard / cost-efficient) while keeping backwards compatibility with existing per-route batching knobs. This is plumbing-only: it defines how tiers and optional per-tier profile overrides are expressed, validated, and surfaced to runtime config resolution. It does not change scheduler heuristics or default knob values; those land in follow-up PRs. Context: As part of workload-aware batching defaults, we need a stable, customer-facing tier concept that can be selected at the route/model policy level and overridden by enterprise/Dedicated configs. We also need to ensure customers with existing manual batching settings do not break, and that invalid or partially-specified configs fail fast with actionable error messages. What’s included: (1) New schema types: - Adds a new enum `BatchingSloTier` with values: `FAST`, `STANDARD`, `COST_EFFICIENT`. - Adds `batching.tier` to `RoutePolicy` (optional). If unset, the effective tier is `STANDARD` (subject to higher-precedence overrides in later work). - Adds `batching.profiles` (optional) for per-tier explicit knob overrides. This is intended primarily for internal benchmarking and Dedicated overrides; public docs will steer customers to tier selection rather than manual knob tuning. (2) Validation + guardrails: - Enforces that per-tier profiles, when present, include required knobs as an all-or-nothing set (prevents partial overrides that can create surprising behavior). - Validates numeric ranges for knob fields (e.g., wait budgets >= 0, token limits > 0) and cross-field constraints (e.g., `max_batch_tokens` >= `max_prefill_tokens_per_request` when both present). - Rejects unknown tier strings with explicit error output listing allowed values. (3) Backwards-compatible defaults / migration path: - Preserves the legacy batching config surface (`max_batch_tokens`, `max_wait_ms`, etc.) for existing customers and internal configs. - Adds a compatibility layer: if legacy knobs are present and `batching.tier` is not set, config resolution treats it as `tier=STANDARD` with an implicit `profiles[STANDARD]` override populated from legacy values (so existing behavior is maintained and the new tier field can be introduced gradually). - If both `batching.tier` and legacy knobs are set, validation fails with a clear message to avoid ambiguous precedence (the explicit guidance is: pick tier OR legacy knobs, not both). (4) Config introspection: - Extends `configctl validate` output to include a normalized view of effective tier + effective profile (for debugging config rollouts and Dedicated deployments). - Adds structured error codes for common validation failures (to support Console UX and customer-facing errors later). Files/areas touched (high level): - `control-plane/config/schema/route_policy.proto` (adds tier and profile fields) - `control-plane/config/validation/batching.go` (validation + compatibility rules) - `control-plane/config/normalize/route_policy_normalize.go` (legacy -> tiered normalized representation) - `tools/configctl/validate_cmd.go` (debug output improvements) - `docs/internal/config-schema.md` (internal-only note; public docs are separate) Example config (normalized): route_policies: - name: chat-default model: llama-3.1-70b batching: tier: FAST profiles: FAST: max_wait_ms: 1 max_batch_tokens: 4096 STANDARD: max_wait_ms: 4 max_batch_tokens: 8192 COST_EFFICIENT: max_wait_ms: 10 max_batch_tokens: 16384 Notes: Values above are illustrative only; final recommended defaults per tier/model/hardware are owned by perf/benchmarking work and will land separately. Testing: - Unit tests for schema validation (valid tiers, invalid tiers, missing required fields, range checks). - Golden tests for normalization output (legacy knobs -> normalized tiered profile). - Ran `configctl validate` against production-like snapshots from Hosted + Dedicated staging (no changes required; legacy configs continue to validate). Rollout plan: - Safe to merge behind existing behavior since tier is optional and legacy configs are untouched. - Follow-ups will: (a) wire tier selection to runtime config consumption, (b) add reason codes/telemetry, (c) add feature flagging and rollout controls. Checklist: - [x] Backwards compatibility confirmed with staging config snapshots - [x] Validation errors are actionable (include field path + suggestion) - [x] Added unit/golden tests - [x] No runtime behavior change in this PR Related: - Linear: ENG-18422 - Project: Workload-aware batching defaults (tiered SLO profiles) Review notes / conversation (abridged): Priya Natarajan: Please ensure we don’t end up with three different places to set batching (legacy knobs, tier, per-tier profiles) without clear mutual exclusivity. Ethan Park: Agreed. Added explicit validation to fail if `tier` is set alongside legacy knobs; also updated normalize logic to only synthesize a `STANDARD` profile when tier is unset. Jordan Lee: Can we avoid `COST_EFFICIENT` vs `COST_EFFICIENT` naming drift across components? Also prefer stable casing in JSON. Ethan Park: Implemented strict enum mapping with JSON strings `fast|standard|cost_efficient` and canonicalized output in `configctl`. Added tests to ensure round-trip stability. Sean Gallagher: Please add guardrails for negative/zero limits; also want error codes for alerting/triage. Ethan Park: Added range checks + `CFG_BATCHING_*` error codes surfaced by `configctl validate` and control plane logs. Approvals: - Priya Natarajan (approved) - Jordan Lee (approved) - Sean Gallagher (approved) CI: build-and-test (pass), configctl-golden (pass), lint (pass). Merge: Squash merged into `main`.

title: Introduce variant-weighted phased gate and explainable failure reporter
preview:
Motivation: We need a gate that can phase-in stricter regression checks across multiple prompt-set variants and weight them by production traffic mix. Existing gates are either binary or treat variants equally, which causes noisy failures from low-impact variants and slow rollouts when a single high-volume variant changes. This PR implements a phased "variant-weighted" quality gate plus an explainable failure reporter that surfaces per-variant contribution and suggested mitigations.

Summary of changes: added a PhaseGate orchestration that accepts variant weights and stage thresholds, a scoring adapter that produces per-variant metrics (accuracy, token-loss, semantic-distance), and an explainability module that generates a short failure report with example prompts, model outputs, and a concise mitigation recommendation. The gate supports three phases (shadow, canary, enforce) and automatically escalates or rolls back based on weighted aggregate score and high-impact-variant signals.

Why this is different: unlike our ensemble or cohort gates this approach natively models traffic-weighted importance, reduces alert fatigue from rare variants, and provides human-readable remediation suggestions to speed incident response. It also integrates with the CI decision buffer to allow staged rollouts during merge checks.

Checklist: - [x] PhaseGate core implementation - [x] Explainable failure reporter - [x] Unit + integration tests - [x] CI hook to evaluate only affected prompt-sets - [x] Telemetry (per-variant metrics and overall weighted score) - [x] Documentation + runbook snippet

Backwards compatibility: The gate is opt-in; existing configuration remains unchanged. Defaults preserve current behavior by uniform weighting and single-phase enforcement.

Operational notes (included in runbook): - The report includes top-5 contributing prompts per-variant and a suggested temporary mitigator (shadow traffic split or pin previous model). - Recommended default weights are derived from a weekly traffic sampler and can be overridden per-linear ticket.

Release notes: add a new "variant-weighted phased quality gate" for eval-harness; provides explainable failure reports and phased rollouts to reduce noisy CI failures and speed triage.

title: Concurrent burst decoupler and safety thresholds for reservation subsystem
preview:
Motivation: In high-concurrency scenarios tenants were simultaneously consuming burst credits and driving reservation drains that caused transient SLA drops. Existing soft-reserve logic treated burst tokens and concurrency as a single coupled signal which amplified scale-down decisions. This PR introduces a concurrent-burst decoupler and tenant safety thresholds to ensure reservation drains are gated by both credit state and observed concurrency pressure.

Summary of changes: This is a focused, backend-only change that touches the reservation allocator, autoscaler decision path, and telemetry surface. Key pieces: 1) decoupler layer that separates short-lived concurrent bursts from sustained reservation usage; 2) tenant safety thresholds (min-reserved-headroom and concurrency-fence) which temporarily block aggressive shrink decisions; 3) util-backpressure estimator that reports a stabilized utilization signal used by the autoscaler; 4) reconciliation loop fix to ensure credits and ledger balances are reconciled after decoupling adjustments to avoid double-counting.

Design notes (high level): - ConcurrentBurstDecoupler: sits between the admission controller and allocator. It observes per-tenant in-flight concurrency and recent token drain rate, and yields a transient allowance token which is charged separately from long-term reservation credits. The goal is to allow brief bursts without triggering reservation contraction. - Safety thresholds: configurable per-tenant knobs: min_headroom_pct and concurrency_fence_ms. When the tenancy under a safety threshold, scale-down decisions are suppressed for a configurable cool-down window. - UtilBackpressureEstimator: computes a smoothed utilization metric (EWMA on per-second samples with percentile clamp) to feed autoscaler hysteresis and avoid reacting to microspikes.

Checklist: - [x] Add decoupler control plane APIs and internal token accounting - [x] Implement tenant safety threshold config and default values - [x] Wire estimator into autoscaler decision path and tests - [x] Add telemetry metrics for decoupled allowance, suppressed_shrinks, and smoothed_utilization - [x] Unit/integration tests for ledger reconciliation - [x] Docs: internal design note and configuration guide (docs/internal/decoupler.md)

Commits (high level): - commit a1f3c8b: introduce ConcurrentBurstDecoupler and basic token flow - commit b2d9a12: add safety threshold config + defaults and gating logic - commit c7e4d90: util_backpressure_estimator + autoscaler integration - commit d4f1b33: reconcile loop fix and ledger compatibility tests - commit e5f2a21: telemetry, e2e smoke tests, and docs

Files changed (high level): - pkg/autoscaler/decoupler.go (+280 additions) - controllers/reservation_allocator.go (+90/+32) - pkg/autoscaler/util_estimator.go (+120 additions) - internal/config/tenant_defaults.go (+35 additions) - telemetry/metrics_defs.yaml (+12 additions) - tests/allocator/decoupler_integration_test.go (+50 additions) - docs/internal/decoupler.md (+20 additions) - scripts/ci/flake_repro.sh (+5 additions) - pkg/ledger/reconciliation_test.go (+? additions)

Small illustrative snippet (decoupler admission check):

if decoupler.AllowTransient(ctx, tenantID, inFlight) {
    // route to transient allowance bucket (does not decrement long-term reservation)
    admitTransient(req)
} else {
    fallbackToReservation(req)
}

Review conversation (selected):
- Ben Zhao (2026-01-21): "Can we make min_headroom_pct a cluster-level override? Some orgs want strict control."
  - Aisha: "Made it cluster override-able. Default still tenant-scoped; added docs and an env var to flip to global enforcement."
- Marco Silva (2026-01-25): "We need a signal that proves the decoupler won't starve long-running requests. Add a metric for transient_exhaustion_rate."
  - Aisha: "Added transient_exhaustion_rate counter and an alerting recommendation in the docs."
- Priya Natarajan (2026-01-28): "Unit tests look good; can we add an e2e that simulates 10 tenants bursting concurrently to show behavior improvement?"
  - Aisha: "Added decoupler_integration_test which simulates mixed burst and sustained load; added deterministic seeds so CI is stable."

CI and validation: Initial CI run had a flaky test in tests/allocator that failed due to timing assumptions; I adjusted the test harness to use a mock clock and re-ran. Final CI status: build: pass, unit tests: pass, integration (smoke): pass, lint: pass, go vet: pass. Coverage delta: +1.3% in autoscaler package.

Telemetry / Dashboards added: - decoupler.allowance_tokens{tenant} gauge - decoupler.suppressed_shrinks counter - autoscaler.smoothed_utilization gauge - ledger.reconciliation_lag gauge

Performance notes: In a synthetic multi-tenant microburst test (10 tenants, 1s bursts every 30s), this change reduced transient SLA violations (p95 latency) by ~38% and reduced noisy scale-down events by ~71% relative to main. Average token cost was unchanged; slight increase in short-lived allowance accounting (~0.6% overhead) but net improvement in availability.

Upgrade notes: Safe to roll to main; the new config defaults are conservative. For Dedicated customers with custom tenant defaults, operators should review min_headroom_pct and concurrency_fence_ms. The decoupler can be disabled via configuration if operators prefer legacy behavior.

Merge: Squash-merged into main after approvals.

title: shifted telemetry compressor and humanized handoff note generator
preview: Motivation: On-call handoffs often arrive with large telemetry attachments and ambiguous next steps. This PR introduces a compact telemetry compressor and a humanized handoff note generator to reduce payload size, remove PII, and provide a cognitive-load estimate and a canary-safe rollback playbook seeded from the collected snapshot. The snapshot collector produces a small 'repro trailer' containing minimal trace slices, top-k logs by error signature, sampled heap/disk metadata and a KV diff bundle. The compressor uses a delta-based rolling window (inspired by rsync-style chunking) plus optional gzip fallback to guarantee size and decompression determinism across platforms. The handoff note generator templates the critical actions, suggested playbook steps, and a short 'operator confidence' metric based on recent similar incidents and successful mitigations. Implementation: adds a new CLI verb incidentctl snapshot gather --compress --seed-playbook and a library package pkg/snapshot with Collector, Compressor and Trailer types. A separate pkg/handoff contains the seeder that turns a trailer into a markdown handoff and an optional canary rollback playbook with explicit verify steps and automated guard rails. Security and privacy: redaction is performed by pkg/sanitize with configurable KMS-backed rules and a strict opt-in flag for payload upload to shared storage. Backwards compatibility: the compressor emits a stable v1 blob header; receivers without decompressor will still get an uncompressed JSON summary. Tests: end-to-end tests simulate 3 incident shapes, compression roundtrip checks, and a small fuzz harness for redaction rules. CI: initial run surfaced a flaky TestHandoffTemplate which was fixed by adding deterministic timestamping in tests. Rationale and impact: expected to reduce average snapshot upload size by 6x for verbose cases, speed up multi-region handoffs, and reduce cognitive overhead for the next responder through structured notes. Commit summary: see commits field. Files changed: see files_changed list. Review: requested tighter PII redaction and explicit opt-in semantics, added audit logs for redaction decisions, added unit tests for compressor edge cases, approved after second iteration. Merge: squash merged to main after CI green and two approvals. Related work: referenced ENG-1423 for handoff UX and SRE-88 for snapshot retention policy. Manual rollout: feature gated behind INCIDENT_SNAPSHOT_V2 and off by default in hosted instance.
