title: Deprecate legacy stream shims; add credential lookup facade and unify SDK iterables
preview: Context: Multiple SDK consumers reported subtle differences in auth lookup timing and streaming iterator shapes across languages. The legacy 'stream shims' layer introduced in earlier rollouts has become a maintenance burden and makes cross-language parity work harder. Motivation: Reduce duplicated auth lookup code, surface a single lightweight facade for credential resolution that all SDKs call into, and simplify streaming APIs to a single async iterator/pull-generator mapping with clearer backpressure hints. What this PR does: 1) Adds a Credential Lookup Facade (sdk/common/cred_lookup) that centralizes token resolution, TTL jitter logic, and prefetch hints used by the Python, TypeScript, and Go SDKs. 2) Reworks streaming surface so each SDK exports a single ergonomic async/pull iterator that maps to the runtime streaming protocol; legacy shims (StreamShimV1) are deprecated and left as compatibility wrappers that call the new facade. 3) Adds a small migration compatibility layer for applications that relied on StreamShimV1 side effects; the layer emits deprecation warnings and forwards calls. 4) Harmonizes default retry profiles for auth failures when streaming (fast-fail then background prefetch) and documents new default presets. 5) Bumps SDK versions and updates changelogs and examples in the quickstarts repo. Testing and validation: unit tests across all three SDKs, end-to-end smoke tests using the live test matrix, and cross-language parity test harness; fixed two flaky tests related to heartbeat timing. Rollout plan: merged to main and will be published behind feature flags for hosted customers for two weeks, then SDK releases will flip the default. Migration notes: existing apps continue to work with the provided shim; consumers should switch to the new iterator surface and call cred_lookup.lookup(tokenHints) for advanced prefetch semantics. Backwards compatibility window: one release cycle for the shim; plan to remove StreamShimV1 in three months with a dedicated deprecation announcement.

title: Introduce deferred-KV-eviction + quant-tier orchestrator and tenant econ profiles
preview: Motivation: long-tail conversational traffic and multi-tenant variability mean the KV cache is frequently evicted from mini-batches even though reusing older KV fragments would save tokens and improve unit economics. Separately, tenant-facing quantization defaults are too coarse: some tenants can accept int8 for background low-SLO routes while others require fp16 for latency-sensitive chat. This PR adds two coordinated pieces: 1) a deferred-KV-eviction policy in the serving runtime that postpones eager KV eviction for low-probability prefix reuse and exposes eviction scoring for per-tenant policies; 2) a quant-tier-orchestrator service + CLI that evaluates unit-econ vs latency tradeoffs and dynamically routes tenants to quantization tiers (fp16, int8, int4-probative) with safety fallbacks. Changes include a lightweight controller that ingests tenant cost/latency targets and historical token shapes, an offline candidate generator to produce per-tenant compact quant profiles, runtime hooks to switch quant mode per-route, telemetry to record econ delta per rollout, and a CLI command (redwood econ profile apply --dry-run) for safe dry runs. Safety: automated rollback if P95 latency increases > 20ms or hallucination regressions detected via evaluation hooks; default fallthrough to fp16 for tenants without profiles. Backwards compatibility: config flags default to off to avoid surprise behavior; operator opt-in required for private/dedicated installs. Checklist: - [x] Implement deferred eviction scoring + runtime hook - [x] Add quant-tier-orchestrator service and API - [x] CLI and presets for tenant profiles - [x] Telemetry + eval hooks for rollback detection - [x] Unit + integration tests and canary rollout plan. Impact: expected unit-econ improvement ~6-15% across sampled tenants in our load test depending on prefix re-use. Related: ENG-1983 (tenant econ objectives), ENG-1991 (quant safety gates).

title: Introduce stream-cadence fingerprint and cost mirroring for observability
preview:
Context and motivation: customer incidents and internal SLO runs showed repeated small latency drifts during streaming responses that were not captured by existing per-request percentiles. These drifts correlate with bursty token emission patterns (short, frequent microbursts) and cross-tenant billing anomalies. This PR introduces two complementary observability primitives: (1) a stream-cadence fingerprint metric that summarizes emission cadence per invocation, and (2) a cost-mirroring pipeline that writes compact per-invocation cost samples to the billing index for reconciliation and anomaly detection.

What I changed (summary):
- Added a lightweight stream-cadence fingerprint generator in src/observability/stream_cadence/fingerprint.go which computes a 64-bit sketch (hash-bucketed inter-token delta histogram) and emits a low-cardinality label (cadence_bucket) + discrete features as metrics. The code is optimized for allocation-free hot path usage.
- Instrumented runtime streaming path (infra/otel/trace_middleware.go) to attach the fingerprint as an attribute and emit a compact microtrace when cadence looks anomalous.
- New Prometheus recording rule and alert (telemetry/prometheus/rules/stream_cadence.yaml) that exposes cadence_bucket histograms and a derived latency-slope SLI for streaming endpoints. Includes a 5m hysteresis window and quiet-window suppression to reduce alert churn.
- Grafana dashboard (dashboards/stream_cadence.json) with a dedicated cadence matrix panel, microburst sparkline, and a cost-reconciliation panel that links to billing samples.
- Billing mirror: scripts/billing/mirror_costs.py implements a batched sidecar that mirrors minimal cost records (invocation_id, model, cadence_bucket, token_count, microcost_units) to the billing index with backpressure controls and tenant-scoped sampling.
- Tests: tests/telemetry/test_stream_cadence.py covers fingerprint distribution and low-cardinality bucketing; added a small e2e scenario in CI to validate the mirror path under load.
- Docs/runbook update (docs/runbooks/stream-cadence.md) describing how to interpret cadences, playbook steps for cadence-related incidents, and suggested alert thresholds.

Files changed (high-level):
- src/observability/stream_cadence/fingerprint.go
- infra/otel/trace_middleware.go
- telemetry/prometheus/rules/stream_cadence.yaml
- dashboards/stream_cadence.json
- scripts/billing/mirror_costs.py
- docs/runbooks/stream-cadence.md
- tests/telemetry/test_stream_cadence.py
- deploy/helm/values-observability.yaml

Small illustrative snippets (kept tiny to stay reviewable):
- fingerprint.go (core loop):
  func updateSketch(deltaMs int64) {
    idx := bucketForDelta(deltaMs)
    sketch[idx]++ // fixed-size array, no heap allocs
  }

- prometheus rule (stream_cadence.yaml):
  - record: obs:stream_cadence:latency_slope_5m
    expr: increase(obs_streaming_latency_sum[5m]) / increase(obs_streaming_latency_count[5m]) - avg_over_time(obs_streaming_latency[5m])

Commit summary (high-level):
- feat(stream-cadence): add fingerprint generator and bucketing
- feat(tracing): attach cadence attributes and compact microtrace emit path
- perf(billing): add cost mirror sidecar with batching/backpressure
- ci(tests): add unit and e2e validation for cadence metrics
- docs: runbook + dashboard export

Review conversation (abridged):
- Mateo Ruiz (review): "Cardinality risk here—how do we bound labels? The sketch and cadence_bucket help, but ensure we don't forward high-card keys to Prometheus or billing. Also add rate limiting to mirror path."
- Author (Priya): "Agree, implemented fixed 16-bucket hash and a top-k suppression for unknown patterns. Mirror path has tenant-level qps gating and adaptive sampling; details in comments."
- Meera Singh (review): "Alert hysteresis looks reasonable; can we add a runbook link in the alert text?"
- Author: "Added runbook URL and example triage steps to the alert annotations."
- Liam O'Connor (review/approve): "Code looks good; verified CI latency tests locally. Two nits on variable naming and a comment. Approved after fixes."

CI and validation: initial CI run reported a flaky e2e failure (test_stream_cadence::test_reconciliation under resource constrained CI). I adjusted the test to mock out network delays and retried; final CI passed (see ci_status). Local perf microbench shows ~0.6ns per token overhead for fingerprinting in hotpath on our sample workload.

Backward compatibility and risk: introduces internal telemetry and a small sidecar process for billing mirroring; both are opt-in via helm values (deploy/helm/values-observability.yaml). No public API changes. The fingerprint uses a fixed bucket count to avoid unbounded label cardinality.

Merge notes: squashed and merged to main after approvals and green CI. Post-merge steps: rollout the mirror sidecar as an opt-in feature flag on a single staging cluster, validate billing reconciliation, then enable staggered rollout across dedicated pools.

Related work: follows up on ENG-2048 (streaming observability gaps) and pairs with ENG-2091 (billing reconciliation pilot). See linked Linear tickets for the full acceptance criteria.

title: unified tool-caller API + delayed-commit retry semantics to stabilize streaming and multi-tool results
preview: Context and motivation: Customers frequently surface two pain points when composing LLM responses with external tools: 1) streaming instability when tool results and model tokens interleave mid-stream, and 2) duplicate side-effects from naive retry semantics for parallel tool calls. This PR introduces a unified 'Tool Caller' API and a delayed-commit retry strategy to address both issues in a developer-friendly, OpenAI-compatible surface. Design summary: The Tool Caller provides a single entrypoint for: a) orchestrating ordered or parallel tool invocations with deterministic merge semantics, b) registering streaming checkpoints so the model can safely stream partial token output without observing incomplete tool side-effects, and c) delayed-commit retries that only expose tool results to the model after idempotency checks pass. Streaming stability is handled via per-tool stream checkpoints plus a small bounded rebuffer window to preserve low-latency streaming while avoiding mid-stream observable state changes. The retry model uses idempotency tokens and a delayed commit phase: tool runs return provisional results that are not yet injected into the model stream; on success and idempotency confirmation we atomically commit the result into the model's context. Implementation highlights: server/api/tool_caller.py defines the ToolCaller class and public HTTP/SDK surface. server/streaming/stream_manager.py implements streaming checkpoints, rebuffer window, and backpressure integration. server/runtime/tool_dispatcher.cpp contains the low-level executor changes for parallel task orchestration and idempotency hooks. SDK shims (sdk/python/redwood/tools.py and sdk/typescript/src/tool-client.ts) expose the new call signature while preserving the old openai-compatible helpers via a compatibility layer. Observability: introduced tool_caller_latency and tool_commit_success metrics and added a small dashboard tile. Tests: new integration test (tests/integration/tool_composition_test.py) covers ordered, parallel, failure+retry, and streaming-snapshot scenarios. CI: added a targeted e2e workflow ci/workflows/tool-e2e.yml. Migration notes: The compatibility layer keeps old helper names; consumers that depended on streaming-mid-tool injections should migrate to the new two-phase commit injection model. See docs/migration/tool-caller-migration.md for a step-by-step guide and sample code. Rollout plan: feature-gate the new API for hosted and dedicated deployments for four weeks, shadow traffic for two days, then enable for 10% of main traffic for a week before full ramp. Monitoring will focus on commit latency, duplicate-effect rate, and streaming rebuffer tail latency. Backout plan: revert feature flag and disable delayed commits; hotfix branch created if we see increased error budgets. Benchmarks: internal perf runs show median end-to-end latency increase of ~6ms for single-tool calls due to idempotency checks, but multi-tool parallel calls saw a 22% reduction in observed duplicate side-effects and a 12% improvement in 99th percentile streaming stability in our multiplexing demo. Checklist: - [x] API spec and SDK shims implemented - [x] Observability added - [x] Integration tests added - [x] Migration guide written - [x] Benchmarks and rollout plan documented QA and verification steps: run example-apps/multiplexing-demo against local stack, validate no duplicate side-effects for simulated retries, run tool-composition integration test under stress, verify CI e2e pass. Security review: idempotency tokens are scoped per-customer and rotated via KMS; we added a short section in docs on token lifecycle. Known limitations: long-running tools that stream their own state must adopt the provisional-result pattern; we added examples in the migration doc. Relationship to other work: builds on ENG-2191 (tool orchestration primitives) and complements ENG-2248 (streaming stability improvements).

title: introduce-path-aware-context-anchors-and-bloom-log-summaries
preview:
Motivation:
We see a recurring pattern where SRE and oncall engineers spend time hunting long-tail incidents that are noisy because traces lack compact contextual anchors. Full log payloads increase cardinality in downstream indices and make alert grouping brittle. This PR introduces a compact, path-aware context anchor service that produces bloom-filter based context summaries (compact anchors) for invocations and attaches them to traces, metrics, and synthetic telemetry. The anchors enable lightweight contextual joins in dashboards and reduce alert noise by enabling fuzzy grouping and de-duplication.

What this PR does (high-level):
- New component: observability-context-bridge (Go microservice) that consumes span events, generates a 128-bit bloom-ish anchor and a short context digest (max 128 bytes) using deterministic keying of sanitized prompt tokens and route path.
- Runtime: a small sidecar integration for the serving runtime to emit context-hash headers; fallbacks to async enrichment when sidecar is disabled.
- Telemetry: Metrics for anchor generation latency, anchor collision rate estimate, enrichment success ratio, and index join hit-rate.
- Tracing: span attribute rwd.context_anchor added; sampled span enrichment joins now use anchor predicates to backfill logs without pushing whole request payloads.
- Dashboards: new dashboard panels (Context Anchor Heatmap, Anchor Collision Trend, Alert-Group Stabilization) plus a compact sparkline for per-route anchor join success.
- Alerts: an adaptive alert curator that uses anchor join fidelity and group stability to de-duplicate noisy alerts; tunable knobs in the Console.

Why bloom-ish anchors?
- Compact: small footprint in traces/metrics and indexes.
- Probabilistic joins are acceptable for our use-case (we only need high-precision low-cost joins for debugging).
- Deterministic keying across service boundaries enables cross-stage correlation without shipping PII.

Backward compatibility and privacy:
- Anchors are deterministic hashes computed over sanitized token fingerprints and route metadata; no raw prompt or PII is stored or transmitted.
- Feature is opt-in per-tenant and disabled by default for Private installs.
- When disabled, spans still carry a minimal flag so the backend can attempt async enrichment if available.

title: introduce-cohort-emulator-with-flame-segmentation-reporter
preview: Context: We often see p95/p99 spikes in the benchmark lab but lack a compact, deterministic way to map tail episodes to kernel-level hotspots across cohorts. This PR adds a lightweight cohort emulator that replays compact multi-session cohorts with controlled timing and a new flame-segmentation reporter that emits compact 'flame segment' artifacts for downstream attribution and visualization. Motivation: Make it possible to reproduce microburst segments deterministically, compare them against a golden set, and quickly iterate on kernel/serving changes without full cluster replays. Scope: emulator core, segmentation reporter, goldset generator, CI perf job integration, docs and an example cohort. Checklist: - [x] Emulator core with deterministic seed and time dilation - [x] Flame-segmentation reporter that emits compact JSON segments and optional pprof links - [x] Goldset generator and compare tool (scripts/generate_goldset.sh) - [x] Perf CI job and threshold gating (bench/ci/perf_job.yaml) - [x] Example cohort and quickstart doc (docs/bench/cohort_emulator.md, examples/cohorts/sample_cohort.json) - [x] Unit tests and an integration perf harness (tests/integration/test_cohort_emulator.py) Limitations: This emulator is intentionally lower-fidelity than our full session replayer — it samples representative KV/cache priming and seq-length mixes rather than reproducing exact traffic. The goal is fast, repeatable reproduction of hotspot segments rather than absolute latency parity. Related: ENG-2198 (cohort diagnostics), ENG-2217 (perf regression tooling).

title: decouple-serializer-from-affinity-and-introduce-cooperative-serialize-queues
preview: Motivation: our telemetry and recent incident traces show a non-trivial CPU-side bottleneck in the request hotpath caused by blocking serialization work that holds thread-affinity (OS-level pinning) and prevents efficient cooperative scheduling between IO, serializer, and runtime workers. This PR separates the concerns: (1) introduce a lightweight cooperative serialization queue that runs on worker threads without requiring permanent thread pinning, (2) decouple the serializer state machine from thread-affinity so short critical sections can opportunistically run on any available worker, and (3) add backpressure signals to the queue which feed into the scheduler to dynamically throttle batch assembly when CPU saturation is detected. Design summary: add runtime/serialize_queue.{h,cpp} implementing a fixed-size ring queue with lock-free handover for small payloads and a CAS-protected overflow path for large messages. Add a SerializerActor that owns transient per-request buffers but is not bound to a specific CPU core; a short move-to-stack and memcpy micro-optimization keeps copies minimal. Thread pool changes: make ThreadPool::pinning optional at task-granularity, introduce ThreadPool::submit_cooperative(task) which participates in the cooperative scheduling loop. KV cache interaction: when a request hits KV cache for a prefix, the cache lookup now signals the serialize queue to short-circuit full marshalling when cached prefix covers >70% of tokens. Rollout strategy: feature-flag guarded, defaults off for Dedicated regions until we run a gated canary. Benchmarks: integration perf runs on our internal perf-lab show median tail (P95) endpoint latency improvement of 9.2% on mixed small/medium requests, median CPU sys-time for request workers fell from 42% to 29% under the same load profile (16 concurrent clients), and end-to-end tokens/sec improved by 12% for continuous-batching workloads due to reduced serialization stalls. Safety and testing: unit tests for the queue and overflow path, stress tests with randomized task yields, and end-to-end perf harness runs included. Compatibility: no API change to external clients. Rollback plan: flip feature flag and revert the small patch in main that wires up the queue. Notes: this is intentionally conservative on the overflow path to avoid introducing GC/alloc churn in the hotpath.

title: Epochal frontier admission prober for soft-horizon continuous batching
preview: Summary: Implements an 'Epochal Frontier Admission Prober' (EAP) for the continuous batching scheduler to reduce starvation for KV-heavy and short-lived requests while preserving throughput for long sequences. Motivation: we've observed recurring tail latency regressions under mixed workloads when aggressive prefix caching and long batching windows are enabled. Existing fixes (shorter windows, conservative admission limits) either hurt throughput or increase cost. EAP introduces a lightweight, epoch-based admission probe that temporarily lowers admission thresholds for requests at the queue frontier, with jittered short leases and a soft-horizon window to prevent repeated preemption. The design is conservative: probes are sampled per-epoch and back off exponentially on contention.

title: scheduler: jittered slot reassignment and KV-warmup throttling
preview: Context: In high-concurrency mixed workloads we observed correlated slot reclamations and KV cache warmups causing transient starvation and p95 latency spikes. This PR implements two complementary runtime controls: (1) jittered slot reassignment to spread slot release/reassignment events across short randomized windows (configurable), and (2) a KV-warmup throttler that staggers cold-KV prefetches and enforces a soft admission budget per-slot to avoid simultaneous heavy prefetches. Changes: Implements a new scheduler helper (jitter_reassign) which inserts small randomized offsets before slot handoff and coordinates with the slot allocator to avoid churn; implements kv_warmup_throttle which tracks per-model warmup tokens and decays budgets based on successful cache hits; exposes scheduler.tuning.jitter_ms and kv.warmup.budget_rate config knobs; updates continuous_batcher admission path to consult warmup budget before immediate prefetch; adds lightweight metrics (jitter.delay_ms distribution, kv.warmup.throttle_rejections) and dashboard panels. Commits: "add jittered slot reassignment helper"; "implement kv warmup token bucket and decay"; "plumb knobs through config and expose metrics"; "perf tests and bench harness for mixed workloads". Changed files (high-level): runtime/scheduler/jitter_reassign.go; runtime/scheduler/slot_allocator.go (small API); runtime/kv/warmup_throttle.go; runtime/batcher/continuous_batcher.go (admission path); metrics/registry.go (new metrics); docs/benchmarks/jitter_warmup_readme.md; tests/perf/test_jittered_reassign.py; tests/unit/test_kv_warmup_throttle.go; ci/workflows/perf.yml. Motivation: Previous approaches (hard timeouts and global cooldowns) either caused head-of-line blocking or were too coarse-grained for multi-tenant bursts. The jittered reassignment spreads out reassignments to reduce synchronized pressure on the slot allocator and downstream kernel selection; warmup throttling prevents cache stampedes and reduces wasted kernel invocations. Performance: On internal perf harness using a 64-GPU cluster and mixed 75% short, 25% long requests we saw median latency change ~+2ms, p95 reduced ~18%, p99 reduced ~10%, and KV cache miss storms reduced by ~42% in burst scenarios. CPU/GPU utilization improved: fewer small kernel startups and more sustained larger kernels resulting in 6% lower cost/throughput token. Testing: unit tests for token decay and edge cases; perf tests covering staggered bursts, tenant fairness, and long-tail KV loads; load test in staging with warmup budget default of 10 tokens/sec. CI: initial perf run showed a flaky bench; reran perf pipeline with increased harness stabilization and all checks passed. Rollout plan: feature-flagged behind runtime.scheduler.jittered_reassign (default enabled for dedicated clusters, opt-in for hosted at 10% rollout), staged ramp: 1% -> 10% -> 50% -> 100% with automated rollback on regression signals (p95 increase >5% or error rates >0.1%). Backout: config toggle and immediate revert PR prepared if needed. Related: references ENG-4219 for prioritized scheduling work, ENG-4291 for KV cache prefetch follow-up. Checklist: design doc attached in Linear; unit tests added; perf benchmark included; docs updated.

title: token-span-attribution-and-adaptive-alert-smoothing
preview:
Motivation:
We currently surface token-level cost and latency aggregations but lack a clear way to attribute SLO impact to specific token spans (e.g. model prompt prefix vs generated tail) and we get frequent alert flapping on percentile SLOs when low-volume, high-cost tails show up. This PR introduces two complementary capabilities: 1) span-level token attribution traces and rollups keyed by token position buckets (prefix/mid/suffix) and cache-affinity (kv-hit vs kv-miss); and 2) an adaptive alert-smoothing layer that reduces noise for route-level token-cost SLOs using a decayed-reservoir quantile estimator that weights tokens by cost and recency.

What this changes (high level):
- Runtime telemetry: emit token-level spans with attributes: token_index, span_bucket (prefix|mid|suffix), kv_affinity (hit|miss), token_cost_units. These are synthesized as aggregated metrics and lightweight trace annotations to avoid high-cardinality explosion.
- Metrics rollups: new Prometheus/OpenMetrics series and Cortex rollup pipeline config to produce: rwd_token_span_cost_sum, rwd_token_span_cost_count, rwd_token_span_latency_p75/p95/p99 per route and per span_bucket.
- Sampling strategy: a token-aware reservoir sampler in the tracing pipeline that biases retention probability proportionally to token_cost_units and recent latency anomalies to preserve informative tails.
- Alerting: SLO rules updated to reference the smoothed quantiles produced by the adaptive estimator; alerting notifications include the top contributing span_bucket breakdown and a compact token-cost histogram snapshot.
- Console: observability UI adds a per-route Token Span panel with heatmap, selectable span_bucket filters, and a toggle to show raw vs smoothed SLO curves for a chosen time window.
- Docs/Quickstart: example notebook showing how to interpret span attribution and how to tune smoothing window for a given workload.

Implementation notes:
- The runtime emits a compact token-span protobuf (rwd.trace.TokenSpan) and a new lightweight counter for kv-affinity to keep cardinality low; the code path in serving/runtime/telemetry is hardened to avoid sync disk writes.
- Reservoir estimator implemented as a fixed-memory sketch using a weighted priority queue and exponential decay; state is materialized at the rollup tier and also emitted as a derivative metric so alert rules can reference it directly without additional query-time compute overhead.
- Backwards compatibility: when normalized flags are off the system falls back to previous token aggregation metrics (no name changes to legacy meters).

- [x] Rollup pipeline config and transformation (observability/rollups)
- [x] Add Prometheus export and Cortex rules (observability/metrics)
- [x] Tracing sampler implementation and unit tests (observability/tracing)
- [x] Console UI panels and integration tests (console/frontend)

We currently surface token-level cost and latency aggregations but lack a clear way to attribute SLO impact to specific token spans (e.g. model prompt prefix vs generated tail) and we get frequent alert flapping on percentile SLOs when low-volume, high-cost tails show up. This PR introduces two complementary capabilities: 1) span-level token attribution traces and rollups keyed by token position buckets (prefix/mid/suffix) and cache-affinity (kv-hit vs kv-miss); and 2) an adaptive alert-smoothing layer that reduces noise for route-level token-cost SLOs using a decayed-reservoir quantile estimator that weights tokens by cost and recency.

What this changes (high level):
- Runtime telemetry: emit token-level spans with attributes: token_index, span_bucket (prefix|mid|suffix), kv_affinity (hit|miss), token_cost_units. These are synthesized as aggregated metrics and lightweight trace annotations to avoid high-cardinality explosion.
- Metrics rollups: new Prometheus/OpenMetrics series and Cortex rollup pipeline config to produce: rwd_token_span_cost_sum, rwd_token_span_cost_count, rwd_token_span_latency_p75/p95/p99 per route and per span_bucket.

- Alerting: SLO rules updated to reference the smoothed quantiles produced by the adaptive estimator; alerting notifications include the top contributing span_bucket breakdown and a compact token-cost histogram snapshot.
- Console: observability UI adds a per-route Token Span panel with heatmap, selectable span_bucket filters, and a toggle to show raw vs smoothed SLO curves for a chosen time window.

title: Introduce invocation segmentation + weighted-error suppressor and segment-level cost/latency metrics
preview:
Motivation: We frequently see noisy error spikes from downstream adapters and third-party integrations that cause alert storming and obscure true regressions. At the same time, token-level cost/latency rollups miss stagewise behavior inside long-running invocations (e.g., prefetch, model-run, post-process). This PR adds an invocation segmentation layer that (1) marks logical stages within an invocation, (2) emits segment-scoped metrics (latency, token-cost, errors), and (3) implements a lightweight weighted-error suppressor so transient noisy components don't overwhelm on-call channels.

Summary of changes: added a runtime segmenter used by the serving pipeline, new metrics (invocation_segment_latency_seconds_bucket, invocation_segment_token_cost_cents, invocation_segment_error_weight), tracing span naming for segments (invocation.segment.{name}), log enrichment with segment_id and segment_index, a Grafana dashboard (Invocation Segment Explorer), and a new SLO/alert policy that uses the weighted-error score and suppression windows. Feature gated under flag `obs.enable_invocation_segmentation` and disabled by default.

Commits:
- a1f3c9c - add core segmenter and simple API for stage boundaries (initial impl)
- b2d5e6a - wire metrics + otel span naming, dashboard JSON, unit tests for registry and histogram buckets
- c3f7aa1 - fix lint/staticcheck failures, add labels to histograms, tighten sampling in tests, update docs snippet

Files changed (high-level):
- src/serving/segmenter.rs - new: core segmenter, lifecycle hooks, in-memory segment context propagation
- src/telemetry/metrics_registry.yaml - new metric definitions and bucket configs
- src/tracing/trace_enricher.go - annotate spans with segment metadata and segment_id
- dashboards/invocation-segment-explorer.json - new Grafana dashboard panels for per-segment latency, token cost sparkline, and weighted-error waterfall
- pkg/alerts/weighted_suppressor.py - new: heuristic suppressor that weights errors by downstream volatility and recent impact
- tests/segmenter_test.rs - unit tests for boundary cases and sampling behavior
- docs/observability/segments.md - user-facing doc example and recommended alert thresholds

Behavior and compatibility: segmentation is opt-in. When disabled, telemetry remains identical to today. When enabled, invocations may emit extra metrics and enriched traces. The segment_id is stable within the invocation scope but not across invocations. The suppressor uses a short-term EWMA to compute volatility and downweights errors from high-volatility endpoints when they exceed configured thresholds.

CI and iteration: initial CI run failed with staticcheck/lint (unused imports and a missing metric label). I fixed the issues in commit c3f7aa1 and re-ran CI. Final status: all checks passed (unit tests, integration smoke, metrics-schema lint, dashboard JSON schema).

Review conversation (abridged):
- Marcus Li (review): "Nice focused change. Two asks: 1) please add a label on histograms for segment_name to make rollups easier; 2) add a deterministic test for segment_id ordering when nested segments are used."
- Ava Chen (author): "Added segment_name label to histogram buckets and a new test that asserts segment_id monotonicity for nested segments (tests/segmenter_test.rs). PTAL."
- Marcus Li: "LGTM after tests. One nit: histogram buckets might be too coarse for sub-10ms segments; consider adding extra low-latency buckets."
- Ava Chen: "Added low latency buckets and adjusted metrics_registry.yaml. No behavioral change; final run passes."
- Priya Natarajan (SRE): "Concern: suppression could hide real regressions if misconfigured. Suggest we expose a per-service override and an emergency bypass flag in Console. Also add alert annotations when suppression is active so oncall can see it."
- Ava Chen: "Added per-service override config, Console bypass flag (ops only), and an alert annotation field that is set when suppression suppressed an alert (docs and dashboard show this)."
- Diego Morales (approver): "Tests + docs look good; approved."

Approvals: Diego Morales approved; Marcus Li approved after fixes; Priya Natarajan approved pending ops bypass which is implemented.

Post-merge operational notes:
- Rollout plan: feature is gated and will be enabled for internal staging first. We'll run for 2 weeks observing (per-segment heatmaps) before enabling for a small set of customers.
- Monitoring: new SLO dashboard and a temporary smoke alert on invocation_segment_emit_rate to detect regressions in instrumentation.
- Runbook: added docs/observability/segments.md with recommended alert thresholds and how to interpret weighted-error scores.

Why this is different from previous work: prior PRs focused on token-slice rollups and span sampling. This PR introduces explicit, programmer-visible segment boundaries and ties them to per-segment telemetry plus an adaptive suppression heuristic to reduce noisy alert churn without blindly muting signals.

Testing notes: unit tests cover segment nesting, metric emission, and suppressor behavior under synthetic volatility. Integration smoke validates dashboard panels render and that trace enrichment is present for the sample invocation.

Merge: Squashed and merged into main on 2025-11-12 after CI green and approvals.

Potential follow-ups (tracked):
- ENG-4390: correlate segment-level token_cost with model-instance hotness for better prefetching cues
- ENG-4422: make suppressor model-aware (decay by model-version)

If you want to run locally: see docs/observability/segments.md for a minimal example that exercises the segment API and emits test metrics to the local metrics sink.

title: Introduce sequence-entropy alignment and token billing lens
preview:
Motivation: Many SRE and billing investigations require correlating the information-theoretic "sequence entropy" signature of a generation request with the downstream billed token cost and latency profile. Existing observability surfaces show per-token cost and coarse prompt shape metrics but do not make it easy to identify when high-entropy sequences are driving disproportionate billing or SLO pressure. This PR introduces a backend metric pipeline and a user-facing dashboard (the 'billing lens') that aligns per-sequence entropy, token-cost attribution, and latency heatmaps so teams can triage high-cost tails faster.

What this change does (single-line description): adds sequence-entropy aggregator, normalized per-tenant cost correlation metric, a Grafana lens dashboard, alert tuning for entropy-driven cost drift, and a small SDK helper for telemetry tagging.

Scope & components changed: backend aggregation (telemetry service) to compute sequence entropy at ingest, a compact per-invocation 'entropy_score' tag propagated through traces, a new rollup used by billing attribution, a Grafana dashboard JSON, an alert rule that suppresses noisy low-volume spikes, and SDK helpers for Python clients to attach entropy hints during local batching.

Checklist:
- [x] telemetry: compute and persist normalized sequence_entropy on invocation ingest
- [x] trace enrichment: attach entropy_score to spans and logs (compact integer bucket)
- [x] metrics: histogram for entropy->token_cost correlation and per-tenant rollups
- [x] dashboard: billing-lens with linking from a selected cohort to raw traces
- [x] alerting: add tunable alert that triggers only when entropy-weighted cost increases and volume > threshold
- [x] SDK: python helper and tests
- [x] docs: observability guide and runbook link

Rollout plan: feature is behind a runtime flag (telemetry.sequence_entropy.enabled). We will enable it for a small set of tenants for 48h and verify that cardinality and ingestion cost are within budget. If signal is noisy, the alert uses the new 'entropy_quiet_window' to automatically mute frequent single-invocation spikes.

Runbooks & links: Linear: ENG-2017 (sequence entropy observability), internal runbook: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-2017, dashboard preview: grafana.redwood.internal/d/entropy-billing-lens (preview).

Privacy & cost considerations: entropy is computed from token logits shapes (no user-visible text is stored). Aggregation uses fixed 8 buckets to bound cardinality. We added metric retention limits and a sampler that retains full traces only for top 0.5% by entropy-weighted cost.

Notes on uniqueness: designed to align an information-theoretic signal (entropy) with billing attributions and SLOs rather than a simple token-count correlation; this helps detect when the same token volume produces different compute costs due to model perplexity and sampling dynamics.

title: Headroom orchestration and differential burst accountant
preview:
Motivation: production tenants exhibit asymmetric load patterns (short microbursts vs sustained ramps). Existing global headroom and simple burst tokens over/under-react, causing either wasted reserved capacity or SLA violations during coordinated microbursts. This PR implements a new headroom orchestration layer that computes per-tenant asymmetric headroom weights and a separate differential burst accountant that decouples short-window burst credits from long-window commitment accounting.

Design summary: the headroom orchestrator consumes sampled utilization histograms from tenant reporters and produces two outputs: (1) dynamic short-window headroom multiplier (reacts to 95th/99th percentile short histograms), and (2) steady long-window buffer (based on 90th percentile over longer horizon). The differential burst accountant maintains two credit buckets per tenant: ephemeral credits (fast refill/leak, used for microbursts) and committed credits (slow refill, backing reserved capacity). Credits are accounted separately and exposed via telemetry with a delta metric so the autoscaler can prioritize replenishing committed credits while permitting ephemeral credits to be served opportunistically.

Config knobs: feature flag `headroom.orchestrator.enabled` (default: off), `orchestrator.short_window_secs`, `orchestrator.long_window_secs`, `accountant.ephemeral_refill_rate`, `accountant.committed_refill_rate`, `accountant.soft_block_threshold_pct`, and `orchestrator.asymmetric_weight_clip`. Defaults chosen conservatively to avoid capacity thrash.

Safety and backwards compatibility: when the feature is disabled the system falls back to existing global headroom and single-token accountant. New metrics and configs are additive; no API changes to the inference gateway. Rollout plan: enable in staging, canary 5% on dedicated fleet, 25% after 24h if no SLA regressions, full rollout after 72h.

Observability: added tenant-level metrics `redwood.headroom.short_multiplier`, `redwood.headroom.long_buffer_bytes`, `redwood.accountant.ephemeral_credits`, `redwood.accountant.committed_credits`, and `redwood.accountant.utilization_delta_percentile_{p}`. Dashboards updated in docs/observability/headroom-metrics.md. Traces include sampling hooks for headroom decisions (to help root-cause size mismatches).

Testing: unit tests for accountant arithmetic and headroom smoothing, integration harness simulating 10 tenants with mixed microburst/sustained patterns, and load test on the canary fleet (see perf numbers below).

Performance results (staging synthetic): with default knobs, we saw 12% fewer SLA breaches on 99p latency during coordinated microbursts and ~8% lower reserved-gpu token-hours used compared to baseline over a 6h window. CPU overhead on the orchestrator pod is ~3% of a single vCPU under 10k tenant sample rate.

Files changed: small focused changes across autoscaler, accountant, metrics, charts, and docs.

Rollout steps included in PR body to enable safe canary and fast rollback if needed.

title: Introduce presubmit cost sampler and toolchain stabilizer
preview: Context: CI noise from transitive dep updates and toolchain version flips has led to intermittent presubmit failures and unpredictable cost spikes in hosted runs. Motivation: reduce noise, gather sample-based cost telemetry, and stabilize the dev toolchain so presubmit failures are actionable rather than transient. What this PR does: 1) Adds a lightweight presubmit-cost-sampler pipeline (ci/presubmit/sampler.yaml) that runs on ~0.5% of PRs and records job-level time/cpu/mem/infra-cost estimates to the internal CI-telemetry store. 2) Introduces a toolchain-stabilizer service (infra/toolchain-stabilizer/) that: - pins a curated minimal set of tool versions for presubmit lanes, - warms language/OS-specific caches on ephemeral runners, - injects a cache-warm header into existing install steps. 3) Adds an opt-in "quiet-merge-window" gating policy (scripts/merge-window/README.md) that batches non-urgent merges during off-peak windows to reduce peak CI contention. 4) Adds a small metrics exporter (tools/ci-metrics/exporter.go) and a docs page (docs/ci/cost-sampler.md) describing cost metrics and how teams interpret them. Backwards compatibility: all changes are opt-in via two repo-level feature flags (repo settings): ci.cost_sampler and ci.toolchain_stabilizer. Default rollout: ci.cost_sampler=false, ci.toolchain_stabilizer=false. Rollout plan: stage 1 (internal): enable both flags for redwood/* small teams (1 week) and validate stability. Stage 2 (canary): enable ci.toolchain_stabilizer=true for 10% of active repos and ci.cost_sampler=true at 0.5% PR sample rate (2 weeks). text: Context: CI noise from transitive dep updates and toolchain version flips has led to intermittent presubmit failures and unpredictable cost spikes in hosted runs. Motivation: reduce noise, gather sample-based cost telemetry, and stabilize the dev toolchain so presubmit failures are actionable rather than transient. What this PR does: 1) Adds a lightweight presubmit-cost-sampler pipeline (ci/presubmit/sampler.yaml) that runs on ~0.5% of PRs and records job-level time/cpu/mem/infra-cost estimates to the internal CI-telemetry store. 2) Introduces a toolchain-stabilizer service (infra/toolchain-stabilizer/) that: - pins a curated minimal set of tool versions for presubmit lanes, - warms language/OS-specific caches on ephemeral runners, - injects a cache-warm header into existing install steps. 3) Adds an opt-in "quiet-merge-window" gating policy (scripts/merge-window/README.md) that batches non-urgent merges during off-peak windows to reduce peak CI contention. 4) Adds a small metrics exporter (tools/ci-metrics/exporter.go) and a docs page (docs/ci/cost-sampler.md) describing cost metrics and how teams interpret them. Backwards compatibility: all changes are opt-in via two repo-level feature flags (repo settings): ci.cost_sampler and ci.toolchain_stabilizer. Default rollout: ci.cost_sampler=false, ci.toolchain_stabilizer=false. Rollout plan: stage 1 (internal): enable both flags for redwood/* small teams (1 week) and validate stability. Stage 2 (canary): enable ci.toolchain_stabilizer=true for 10% of active repos and ci.cost_sampler=true at 0.5% PR sample rate (2 weeks). Stage 3 (gradual): increase sampler to 2% and stabilizer to 50% if metrics are green. title: Introduce policy simulator with KMS proofing and audit-diff tooling
preview: Title: Introduce policy simulator with KMS proofing and audit-diff tooling. Motivation: Customers and internal auditors frequently ask for a way to validate RBAC v2 policy changes before rollout and to produce cryptographic proof that a proposed change does not widen region/KMS residency exposure. This PR adds a developer-facing simulator (rbac-sim) that evaluates a set of example requests against a candidate policy delta, emits a human- and machine-readable audit-diff, and (optionally) attaches a short-lived KMS-signed proof bundle that attests to the simulator inputs and outputs for downstream compliance workflows. This is intended to reduce post-deploy surprises, speed review cycles for Dedicated/Private deployments, and plug into our audit retention processes. Summary of changes (high level commits): 1) feat(simulator): introduce rbac-sim CLI and library with JSON policy delta apply and evaluation harness — high-level API, test suite and example fixtures (commit 1/6). 2) feat(audit-diff): add audit-diff generator that produces compact ndjson with decision-level deltas and counts for each service/operation combination (commit 2/6). 3) feat(kms-proof): add optional KMS proof signer that wraps the simulator run in a signed envelope (supporting both hosted KMS and customer CMKs via delegation tokens) (commit 3/6). 4) chore(ci): add unit and integration tests to workflow and container build step (commit 4/6). 5) docs: add quickstart and compliance playbook describing how to run sims against production policy snapshots and how to consume proof bundles (commit 5/6). 6) perf: small perf improvement to policy compile cache to keep simulator runs under 300ms for typical policy sets (commit 6/6). Changed areas/files (high level): - services/rbac/simulator/{main.go,simulator.go,policy_parser.go,fixtures/} - tools/rbac-sim/dockerfile and Makefile - libs/kms/proof_signer.go with integrations for hosted KMS and delegated CMK tokens - infra/ci/rbac-sim.yml (unit + policy integration tests) - docs/compliance/rbac-simulator.md and examples/compliance/playbook.md - testdata/policy-deltas/* (example deltas used in CI) Review notes and design decisions: - Simulator semantics intentionally mirror production PDP evaluation order; we reuse policy compile and the PDP cache. Edge cases for resource wildcards were clarified and documented. - KMS proofing is opt-in; the envelope contains a compact SHA256 of the input fixtures and the ndjson audit-diff, a timestamp, and a signer id. Proofs use existing hosted KMS signing key by default; for Dedicated/Private customers we accept a delegated CMK token and include the key fingerprint in the envelope metadata. - We modeled the audit-diff format around existing ndjson conventions used by the observability team so the output can be ingested into our log-retention and compliance pipelines without extra transformation. - Security review: ephemeral tokens for delegated CMK usage are validated against operator-scoped grants; we added additional guardrails to prevent simulator-initiated KMS requests from being used as an escalation vector. Testing and CI: - Unit tests added for policy parser edge cases, comparator and ndjson generation. - Integration test runs in CI spin up a minimal policy store and runs three representative deltas; artifacts are validated (ndjson schema + optional proof envelope signature verification). - CI checks passed on final run: build (pass), unit-tests (pass), integration-tests (pass), lint (pass). Review conversation (condensed into single timeline): - Ethan Chu (reviewer): "Nice to see a simulator — two asks: 1) make sure the simulator cannot call production KMS by default; 2) add a gated flag for proofing so that automated CI jobs don't accidentally sign artifacts with hosted keys." - Author (Priya): "Both addressed — default is dry-run-only and proofing requires explicit --enable-proof + env var pointing to KMS key alias. Added tests to assert the default behavior." - Marisol Vega (reviewer): "We should include a short retention policy recommendation for proof bundles — added a doc comment." - Samir Patel (security reviewer): "Verified token validation logic and put a small nit on log noise; asked for an additional audit check that the simulator writes a non-replayable nonce into the envelope." - Priya: "Added nonce and updated docs; also added an integration test that verifies nondeterminism across runs with the same inputs when nonce is present." - Merge readiness: reviewers approved after two rounds of fixes. CI green on approval. Observability and ops notes: - Simulator runs emit metrics under rbac.simulator.run.{success,failure,duration_ms} and proofing emits rbac.simulator.proof.sign.{success,failure}. - Console: added a minimal dashboard tile for recent simulator runs (docs include Grafana snippet). - Rollout plan: feature behind a feature flag rbac.simulator.enabled; default disabled in Hosted. Admins can enable per-tenant for Dedicated and Private installs. Compliance playbook: - How to run: ./tools/rbac-sim --policy-snapshot /path/to/snapshot --delta /path/to/delta.json --out audit-diff.ndjson --enable-proof --kms-key=alias/custom-cmk - How to review: the audit-diff groups changed allow/deny decisions by service and includes sample example requests that would change decision. Proof bundle contains base64(signed_envelope)+ndjson and a verification helper script for auditors. Migration and operational impact: - No runtime changes to PDP; simulator is a standalone tool and library. - Policy changes should still go through standard Linear flow; engineers are encouraged to attach audit-diff + proof to policy change PRs. - For Dedicated/Private customers, operator docs include steps to provide CMK delegation tokens and to configure short-term key usage. Security mitigations implemented: - Simulator KMS operations are rate-limited and annotated; we added a sandbox KMS path for Hosted to avoid accidental use of production keys in automated workflows. - Proof envelopes include signer id, key fingerprint, timestamp, nonce and a tight TTL (default 24h). - Added RBAC rule to prevent non-admin roles from creating proof-enabled simulator runs through the API wrapper. Known limitations and future work: - Current simulator validates policy deltas only; future work will support side-by-side multi-tenant scenarios where composite policy resolution is necessary. - We do not yet automatically attach simulator artifacts to Linear tickets; that will be a small follow-up (ENG-4369). Merge outcome: merged via squash/merge after approvals; release note drafted and added to changelog. Additional metadata: example command lines, proof envelope format and verification helper included in docs. Contact: priya@redwood.example for follow-ups.

title: Introduce contextualized minibatch regression watchdog and suppression adapter
preview:
Motivation: Flaky per-prompt regressions are noisy in CI when single minibatch failures trigger rollbacks or high-severity alerts. This PR adds a contextualized minibatch regression watchdog that computes short-horizon, context-aware regression signals and a suppression adapter that dampens alerts from transient minibatch noise. The goal is to reduce false positives in CI gating while preserving sensitivity to sustained quality regressions.

What this PR introduces: 1) src/watchdogs/contextual_minibatch_watchdog.py - new watchdog which calculates per-prompt-context rolling statistics (short window mean, variance, exponential smoothing) and emits a scored regression signal. 2) src/adapters/suppression_adapter.py - a pluggable adapter used in CI and alerting pipelines to apply hysteresis and suppression rules: confirmation windows, adaptive thresholds based on context prevalence, and a backoff multiplier for high-variance prompts. 3) src/metrics/contextual_variance_aggregator.py - a lightweight aggregator that computes cross-seed/context variance and integrates with existing EvalMetric interface. 4) ci/hooks/watchdog-ci.yml - CI hook that runs a focused smoke eval using minibatch shards and gates on sustained signal (configured as 3 consecutive windows). 5) tests/{unit,integration}/watchdog_* - unit and integration tests covering edge cases (all-zero tokens, variable batch sizes, missing contexts), plus a flakiness simulator for synthetic load.

Design notes: - The watchdog uses context keys derived from prompt metadata (domain, prompt_type, client_tier) so statistics are computed in semantically meaningful buckets. Bucketing is configurable at runtime. - Suppression adapter policy: require confirmation over N windows or N distinct shards before escalating. N defaults to 3 but is policy-driven per repo/route. - CI hook semantics: the CI job will fail only if the suppression adapter permits escalation. The default CI configuration is conservative (designed to reduce developer churn) but project leads can opt-in to stricter thresholds.

Compatibility and rollout: - Backwards compatible: new modules expose adapter hooks but do not change existing evaluation behavior unless enabled via config. - Rolling deployment plan: (a) land code behind feature flag, (b) enable on small set of noisy prompt-sets for 2 weeks, (c) expand to all smoke CI jobs if no missed regressions detected.

Checklist (manual): - [x] Unit tests for watchdog logic and aggregator - [x] Integration test that simulates transient flakiness and verifies suppression - [x] CI job definition and README for ops - [x] Documentation note in docs/eval/watchdogs.md (referenced in PR body)

Notes: This PR focuses on detection and suppression for minibatch-level noise patterns. It intentionally avoids hard-coded model-level thresholds and instead exposes configuration knobs for per-team policy tuning.

title: Add binding inheritance guard and geo-key provenance header
preview: Summary: This PR adds a binding inheritance guard to RBACv2, introduces a lightweight KMS provenance header (X-KMS-Origin-Chain) surfaced through the gateway, and a small audit consolidator used by the audit-forwarder to deduplicate events coming from chained proxies. Motivation: We observed two related classes of compliance gaps in multi-zone customers: (1) operator/service bindings that unintentionally inherited privileges when tenant objects were copied across geozone boundaries and (2) duplicated/misaligned audit entries from edge proxies that obscured key provenance. The changes here are intended to close those gaps with minimal disruption and a staged rollout. What changed (high level): - rbac/policy-compiler: add BindingInheritanceGuard module. The guard enforces that operator/service role bindings cannot be transitively inherited across geozone boundaries unless an explicit 'inherit: true' attribute exists on the target namespace and the tenancy claim includes an allowlist. - gateway/proxy: attach X-KMS-Origin-Chain header (comma-separated list of key-region:provider entries) to internal KMS calls and to outgoing audit envelopes when present. The header is signed by the gateway's operator key when crossing zone boundaries. - audit/forwarder: introduce AuditConsolidator which deduplicates events using (trace_id, request_id, key_provenance_hash) and tags consolidated events with provenance-summary for downstream retention/policy. - admission controller: validate new binding attributes on create/update and reject suspicious cross-zone binds in enforced mode. - policy migration: tooling to scan and annotate namespaces with 'inherit' flags and produce a remediation report. - tests: unit and integration tests for guard, header propagation tests under fault injection, and end-to-end audit consolidation test harness. Rollout plan: 1) Shadow mode (default): BindingInheritanceGuard runs in 'observe' mode, emitting structured telemetry to SLO/alerts and adding findings to the policy-migration report. 2) Canary enforcement: enable enforcement in a small set of non-production tenants for 72h. 3) Gradual opt-in: expose a tenant opt-in flag and run a migration job to annotate safe namespaces. 4) Full enforcement: after 4 weeks of canaries and zero high-severity findings, flip default to 'reject' for newly created cross-zone binding attempts. Backwards compatibility: X-KMS-Origin-Chain is optional. Old gateways without the header continue to operate; the consolidator treats missing provenance as 'unknown' and only applies dedupe heuristics with reduced confidence. Security review notes: The header is canonicalized and signed to avoid tampering; gateway signs using operator's zone key. Audit envelopes containing provenance are stored with a provenance hash to simplify forensic queries. Related work: references ENG-2247 (rbac policy hygiene) and ENG-4902 (kms provenance telemetry). See also SUP-78 (support ticket documenting customer incident). Migration steps included in the PR ensure we do not inadvertently block legitimate operator workflows. The description below includes the checklist and a short runbook for rollback. Checklist: - [x] Policy compiler change unit tests - [x] Gateway header propagation integration tests - [x] Audit consolidate integration tests - [x] Admission controller rejection tests - [x] Migration scanning tool and dry-run report - [x] Documentation updates (RBACv2 policy guide + operational runbook) Runbook (quick): - To disable enforcement quickly set ENV RBAC_BINDING_GUARD_MODE=observe on policy-compiler service and feature flag redwood.rbac.binding_guard=false in config-service. - To remove header propagation set GATEWAY_DISABLE_KMS_PROVENANCE=true and restart canaries. - To revert audit consolidation, flip AUDIT_CONSOLIDATOR_ENABLED=false and reprocess queued envelopes.

title: introduce-historical-variance-detector-and-variance-gate-hook
preview:
Motivation: We've seen intermittent metric oscillations across repeated eval runs (same prompt set, different seeds / sampling windows) that cause nondeterministic gate failures and noisy postdeploy regressions. This PR introduces a Historical Variance Detector (HVD) to detect when a prompt-set or cohort exhibits unstable score distributions over a sliding window of previous eval runs and optionally block merges.

What I changed (high level):
- Add a new module eval_harness/variance_detector.py that computes per-prompt and cohort-level variance, runs a two-sample KS test against the historical distribution, and produces a compact variance report.
- Add an opt-in GH Action workflow .github/workflows/variance-gate.yml that invokes the harness check with the new --variance-check flag on PRs targeting main and release/*.
- Extend the CLI: `harness check --suite <suite> --variance-check --lookback 8 --alpha 0.01 --delta-threshold 0.12` (defaults chosen to be conservative). The CLI returns non-zero if any cohort/prompt exceeds thresholds.
- Integrate an experimental `rollback_scorer` that surfaces the top-N prompts/cohorts contributing to variance; this hooks into the existing rollout recommendations UI (backend only) so SRE/Platform can inspect before rolling forward.
- Tests: add unit tests for distribution comparisons and an integration test that runs against a synthetic history store. Also add a small compatibility shim for older historical-run artifacts.

Commits (summary):
- c1f8b2e: initial variance-detector implementation + docs
- 7a2d9f4: add KS test and sliding-window logic; expose lookback/alpha flags
- e3b4a01: GH Action workflow for variance gate + small CI adapter
- 9f0a5c7: integrate rollback_scorer and add API surface for console
- f1d3b6a: tests and flaky-seed fix (see review notes)

Files changed (high level):
- eval_harness/variance_detector.py (+320 additions)
- eval_harness/cli.py (+18 additions) — new flags and wiring
- integration_tests/test_variance_gate.py (+42 additions)
- .github/workflows/variance-gate.yml (+45 additions)
- docs/variance.md (+30 additions)
- eval_harness/history/shim_compat.py (+20 additions)
- eval_harness/rollback_scorer.py (+35 additions)

Design notes and rationale: HVD is deliberately conservative: it only gates when both the normalized variance and a KS-test p-value indicate distributional shift beyond the delta threshold. This reduces false positives from isolated outliers. The sliding window defaults to 8 most recent eval runs (empirically chosen from internal history), but is configurable per-suite. We log compact histograms and top-contributors to make triage actionable.

Review conversation (abridged, chronological):
- Ethan Cole: "Can the detector be made resilient to differing sample sizes? If historical runs have different numbers of completions we should normalize or bootstrap." PRIYA: "Implemented sample-size-aware bootstrapped CIs in the latest commit; added integration test to cover uneven sizes." (commit 7a2d9f4)
- Maya Chen: "Please parameterize lookback and alpha at the suite level in addition to CLI flags; also document expected run cadence." PRIYA: "Added suite-level defaults in suite_config and updated docs/variance.md with recommended cadences." (commit e3b4a01)
- Luca Bianchi: "Flaky integration test: failing intermittently on CI due to RNG. Can we seed or use deterministic sampling?" PRIYA: "Fixed by seeding the synthetic generator and switching from random.gauss to numpy's Generator with PCG64 for deterministic draws in tests." (commit f1d3b6a)
- Maya Chen: "Approving — please squash on merge; would like a follow-up to wire alerts into Slack ops channel." (approval)

This PR introduces a Historical Variance Detector (HVD) to detect when a prompt-set or cohort exhibits unstable score distributions over a sliding window of previous eval runs and optionally block merges.

What I changed (high level):

- Add an opt-in GH Action workflow .github/workflows/variance-gate.yml that invokes the harness check with the new --variance-check flag on PRs targeting main and release/*.
- Extend the CLI: `harness check --suite <suite> --variance-check --lookback 8 --alpha 0.01 --delta-threshold 0.12` (defaults chosen to be conservative). The CLI returns non-zero if any cohort/prompt exceeds thresholds.

- Tests: add unit tests for distribution comparisons and an integration test that runs against a synthetic history store. Also add a small compatibility shim for older historical-run artifacts.

title: Cost‑Smarts: telemetry-driven prefill scheduler and quantization hint adapter
preview:
Context and motivation: Large parts of our cost-automation stack rely on static prefill and quantization defaults that are tuned per-model but not per-workload. Customers running mixed telemetry (short chat bursts + long completion bursts) see suboptimal unit economics because prefill and quant decisions are not being informed by runtime signals. This PR introduces 'Cost‑Smarts' — a telemetry-driven prefill scheduler plus a quantization hint adapter that surfaces unit-econ deltas into the serving runtime policy engine.

What this change does: Implements a small, runtime-side scheduler that consumes existing request telemetry (sequence length histograms, prefix-kv hit ratios, tail-latency percentile) and emits short-lived prefill decisions (aggressive/standard/disabled) for a given route+model tuple. In parallel, the quantization hint adapter exposes a per-route score representing the estimated per-token cost uplift vs quality risk, which the router can use to select a compatible quantized variant when under cost pressure. Both components default to conservative behavior and are configurable from the runtime config and via the console.

Design highlights and tradeoffs: The scheduler is intentionally lightweight — it aggregates telemetry in 10s windows and uses rule-based heuristics derived from recent econ experiments (prefill when median prefix length > 16 and kv-hit < 0.6, or when 95p latency < configured threshold). We intentionally avoid ML models for initial rollout to keep behavior explainable and safe. The quant hint adapter computes a rollout-friendly score using a small lookup table based on model family, qprofile, and observed batch sizes; the score is a float in [0.0, 1.0] representing 'cost-favorability'.

Checklist: - [x] Runtime scheduler + config knobs - [x] Quant hint adapter + API contract with router - [x] Console defaults/UI flag (defaults panel) - [x] End-to-end unit and integration tests - [x] Telemetry metrics + dashboards - [x] Docs and migration notes

Files and high-level changes: Added a new runtime module runtime/prefill_scheduler.rs implementing the aggregator and decision API; runtime/quant_hint_adapter.rs implements the lightweight scoring; modified router/policy_router.go to accept quant_hint scores when selecting a variant; console defaults UI tweak in console/src/routes/costDefaults; new telemetry metrics in observability/metrics.yaml and a Grafana dashboard JSON in observability/dashboards/prefill_cost_smarts.json. Tests added under runtime/tests and router/integration.

Safety and rollout: Defaults are conservative. The system starts in passive mode (observing only) unless enabled by a new env var PREFILL_SCHEDULER_MODE=active. New config 'costSmarts' allows per-tenant opt-in and pinning of decisions. We added automatic fallback to existing prefill/quant defaults in all code paths.

Performance and unit economics: Internal benchmarks on a mixed workload trace (chat + long completions) show a 7–12% reduction in per-token GPU seconds when cost-smarts is active + quant variants available. Tail latency impact was within measurement noise in 95th percentile tests when prefill choices leaned conservative.

Migration notes: Operators can enable cost-smarts per-tenant via the console or config. No changes needed to existing customer configs unless they want to opt in to aggressive prefill or quant-favoring behavior.

Related work: complements recent econ autotuners (ENG-2201) and the prefill-prober; differs by being runtime-native and telemetry-driven rather than offline profile-based.

How to review: Start with runtime/prefill_scheduler.rs for decision logic, then router/policy_router.go for selection changes. Console changes are small and behind a feature flag. Tests include unit coverage for heuristics and an integration scenario that replays a production trace.

Summary: Implements a safe, incremental path to using live telemetry to improve prefill/quant decisions and lower per-token cost without changing public inference APIs.

title: tenant-latency-bucketed-reservation-and-surge-thresholds
preview:
Summary: Adds a tenant-level latency-bucketed reservation system and a configurable surge threshold gate to Dedicated fleet admission and the autoscaler pipeline. The change introduces a small, opt-in admission controller that tracks recent latency percentiles per-tenant, maps requests into latency buckets (p0, p50, p90, p99), and enforces soft reservations per-bucket. When a tenant exceeds its budgeted bucket, requests are conditionally routed to a surge queue with rate-limited admission; if the surge queue fills, requests are gracefully degraded or routed to an overflow model variant based on policy.

Motivation: We observed repeated incidents where single-tenant tail latency spikes (p99) triggered autoscaler upsizing and burst credits consumption while average-throughput remained low. This caused suboptimal capacity churn and degraded multi-tenant fairness. The latency-bucket model lets us reason about and reserve headroom for critical latency tiers while still allowing controlled bursts above the reservation. It reduces reactive autoscaler upsizing from tail noise and gives the operator explicit surge controls.

Design highlights: - Latency buckets: p0 (best-effort), p50, p90, p99. Tenants declare a target distribution and an overall reservation budget. The admission controller maintains an exponential-weighted moving percentile estimator per bucket. - Reservation accounting: soft credits allocated per-bucket; credits decay with time and refill at tenant-specific rates. Credits are consumed on admitted requests; burst consumption is charged against a surge allowance. - Surge threshold gate: a configurable threshold (utilization or absolute concurrent requests) triggers surge gating. When gate open -> allow spike up to ephemeral surge allowance; when closed -> divert to overflow or enforce throttling. - Autoscaler integration: autoscaler now consumes aggregated bucketed-utilization signals rather than only raw QPS/CPU. It uses a hysteresis policy tuned to ignore isolated p99 spikes when they do not align with sustained bucket utilization. - Telemetry: new metrics per-tenant: tenant.latency_bucket.{p0,p50,p90,p99}.utilization, tenant.reservation.credits, tenant.surge.fill_ratio, tenant.surge.admissions, fleet.bucketed_utilization_percentiles. Added tracing spans for admission decisions and charge attribution. - Config surface: YAML knobs for Dedicated: per-tenant reservation profile, surge allowance, refill rates, p99 hysteresis window, overflow model mapping. Exposed via Console and CLI for operators.

Commit summary: - feat(admission): add latency bucket estimator and per-tenant ledger (7 commits) - feat(autoscaler): consume bucketed signals and implement hysteresis decider - feat(telemetry): emit new tenant-level metrics and dashboard queries - test: unit tests for estimator, integration test harness updates for multi-tenant microburst scenarios - docs: operator guide and console UI knobs explained

Files changed (high level): - services/admission/controller.go (new admission flow + attach points) - packages/reservations/ledger.go (credit accounting, refill logic) - autoscaler/decider/bucketed_decider.go (hysteresis + decision logic) - observability/metrics/tenant_buckets.prom (metric definitions and labels) - ci/integration/microburst_harness_test.go (new integration scenarios) - docs/operations/dedicated_reservations.md (operator guide) - web/console/src/pages/dedicated/tenantProfiles.tsx (console controls)

Small illustrative diff snippets (high-level): - reservations/ledger.go: added DebitBucket(tenantID, bucket, weight) and RefillLoop() with time.Ticker-backed refill. - autoscaler: replaced rawQPS signal with bucketedUtilSignal.Aggregate(window) and applied sigmoid-backed hysteresis.

Tests and CI: Unit tests added for percentile estimator and ledger edge-cases (credit exhaustion, refill race). Integration harness runs a 20k request synthetic microburst across 50 tenants and asserts fleet-level p99 tail is within targets while ensuring no tenant consumes >3x allotted surge allowance. CI showed a transient flake in integration which was fixed by increasing test harness timeouts; all checks green on final run.

Review conversation: - Ethan Morales (reviewer): "Overall clean design. A couple of questions: 1) how is the refill rate tuned for tenants with steady vs spiky workloads? 2) can operators observe charge attribution per route?"
- Priya (author): "Refill rates default to a conservative per-minute token refill based on historical average QPS; we expose per-tenant override in the YAML profile. Charge attribution is emitted via tenant.surge.admissions with route label — added to telemetry and dashboard in this PR."
- Lina Chen (reviewer): "Need stronger safety when ledger storage falls behind (e.g., control plane partition). Propose fail-open for a rolling window and escalate to ops alert; otherwise risk widespread throttling."
- Priya: "Added a fail-open window (configurable 30s default) and an ops alert metric tenant.reservation.stale_detected. Documented behavior in ops guide."
- Marcus Rivera (approver): "Approved after tests and ops docs. Verified dashboard queries and that autoscaler does not react to isolated p99 blips in staging."

CI summary: - unit: pass - integration: pass (rerun once) - lint: pass - e2e-console: pass

Deployment notes: Rollout is gated behind a feature flag per-dedicated-cluster. Default configuration is non-invasive (all tenants soft-unlimited) and operators can opt in per-tenant. We plan a gradual rollout: enable for a pilot set of 5 tenants, validate metrics for 48h, then expand. Console UI controls are behind same flag.

Rollout validation checklist (in PR description for operator runbook): - enable feature on pilot cluster - create two tenant profiles (steady and spiky) and set reservations - run microburst harness to validate p99 isolation - monitor tenant.surge.fill_ratio and tenant.reservation.credits - if unexpected throttling: increase burst allowance or fail feature off

Related work and follow-ups: - ENG-7340: integrate with Commitment Safety Reservoir to provide cross-tenant hedging for long surges - ENG-7259: UI for visualizing per-route charge attribution - follow-up: tune autoscaler hysteresis constants with 2 weeks of pilot data

Merge outcome: Squash-merged into main. Release notes added to changelog.

Why this is different from earlier PRs: Earlier work focused on utilization and burst token pools; this PR introduces latency-aware reservations so we can separate throughput-driven capacity decisions from tail-latency driven transient spikes. It complements existing burst banking approaches by adding a latency signal and explicit surge gating policy.

Contact: Priya Natarajan (author) for implementation details; Lina Chen for ops validation and dashboard queries.

title: Signal router: add core metrics + dashboard hooks (created_signals, routed_tasks, HubSpot errors, lag)
preview: Context: For the PLG-to-sales signal router, we need first-class visibility into end-to-end health (signal creation volume, HubSpot routing volume, HubSpot error rates, and pipeline lag) before expanding the pilot cohort. This PR instruments the signal-router service with a small set of stable metrics and wires them into our dashboards/alerts scaffolding so Oncall + BizOps can see regressions quickly. Linked: ENG-7824 (dashboards/alerts for pipeline health). Related: INT-1843 (HubSpot 429s blocking task creation). What’s in this PR: (1) Service metrics (Prometheus): created_signals_total: counter; incremented when we persist a new PLG Signal (post-dedupe) (labels: env, signal_type, cohort). routed_tasks_total: counter; incremented on successful HubSpot task creation (labels: env, task_type, signal_type). hubspot_429s_total: counter; incremented when HubSpot responds 429 (labels: env, endpoint). hubspot_5xx_total: counter; incremented when HubSpot responds 5xx (labels: env, endpoint, status_code). lag_seconds: histogram; measures time from analytics row watermark to successful HubSpot write (labels: env, pipeline_stage). (2) Structured logs: add per-run summary log line (counts + p50/p95 lag) and include hubspot_request_id where available. (3) Dashboard hooks: add a dashboard JSON + queries for the above metrics (Grafana), with standard Redwood panel templates and placeholders for NA pilot filters. (4) Alerts (scaffold only): add alert rules (initially muted in prod) for sustained hubspot_429s rate and lag_seconds p95 above threshold; these are shipped but disabled pending pilot tuning. Notes/decisions: - Kept label cardinality intentionally low (no company_id/org_id labels) to avoid Prometheus blowups; per-account triage stays in logs/HubSpot timeline. - Used histogram for lag_seconds (not gauge) to make p95/p99 alerting reliable. - hubspot_5xx_total includes status_code label; 429 is split out explicitly since it’s operationally distinct and we already track INT-1843. How to test: - unit tests validate metric increments on success/failure paths (task creation, property upsert, note append). - local: run signal-router with HUBSPOT_BASE_URL pointed to mock server; hit /__debug/metrics and confirm counters/histogram show up. - staging: replay a small backfill window; verify Grafana dashboard populates and lag distribution looks sane. Rollout plan: - merge behind existing feature flags (no change to routing decisions). - enable dashboards for NA pilot namespace. - keep alerts muted until we collect 1 week of baseline data. Follow-ups: - ENG-7871 (new): unmute alerts + set thresholds after week-1 baselines. - Consider adding a retry_queue_depth gauge once queueing lands (see ongoing HubSpot batching work).

Notes/decisions: - Kept label cardinality intentionally low (no company_id/org_id labels) to avoid Prometheus blowups; per-account triage stays in logs/HubSpot timeline. - Used histogram for lag_seconds (not gauge) to make p95/p99 alerting reliable. - hubspot_5xx_total includes status_code label; 429 is split out explicitly since it’s operationally distinct and we already track INT-1843. How to test: - unit tests validate metric increments on success/failure paths (task creation, property upsert, note append). - local: run signal-router with HUBSPOT_BASE_URL pointed to mock server; hit /__debug/metrics and confirm counters/histogram show up. - staging: replay a small backfill window; verify Grafana dashboard populates and lag distribution looks sane. Rollout plan: - merge behind existing feature flags (no change to routing decisions). - enable dashboards for NA pilot namespace. - keep alerts muted until we collect 1 week of baseline data. Follow-ups: - ENG-7871 (new): unmute alerts + set thresholds after week-1 baselines. - Consider adding a retry_queue_depth gauge once queueing lands (see ongoing HubSpot batching work).

title: Add signal suppression + false-positive guardrails (internal org filtering, min-volume gates)
preview: Context: During the NA pilot we’re seeing a non-trivial number of “vanity” intent triggers and a couple of true false positives (notably Quartz Commerce) resulting in unwanted outreach (see SUP-3392). This PR adds a set of guardrails to reduce noise while keeping recall for genuinely sales-worthy signals. Summary of changes: (1) Suppression windows: prevent re-routing the same signal type for the same account within a configurable cooldown (default 14d for intent, 7d for reliability/usage) unless severity increases. We store last_routed_at by (workspace_id, signal_type) and compare against current signal_score + severity. (2) Internal/test org filtering: blocklist/allowlist logic for Redwood-owned workspaces and known test tenants; also filters “example.com” style seeded domains and sandbox workspaces tagged by billing. This is applied before scoring so we avoid polluting analytics tables and HubSpot with internal activities. (3) Minimum-volume gates: require minimum sustained volume for threshold signals (tokens/spend/429s) to reduce one-off spikes; add a separate gate for “docs intent only” that requires either (a) 2+ distinct sessions across 48h or (b) docs intent + at least one supporting product event (workspace invite spike, API key rotation, or repeated rate-limit). (4) Confidence floor: enforce a minimum confidence threshold for routing (default 0.65) with per-signal overrides, and write suppressed candidates to a side table for tuning. (5) Auditability: whenever we suppress we emit a structured log and a metric with reason codes (cooldown, internal_org, min_volume, confidence_floor). Tradeoff notes: This will reduce total routed tasks (expected 25–40% drop based on pilot cohort replay), but should materially reduce spam/false positives. We explicitly bias toward fewer tasks while we tune thresholds; suppressed events remain queryable for later adjustment. Testing: Added unit tests for suppression keying + cooldown behavior, internal org matching, and min-volume gating; added a replay test against a sanitized subset of the pilot cohort to validate volume impact. Follow-ups: (a) Add UI surfacing of “suppressed signals” for triage (see DES-412), (b) Re-evaluate doc intent heuristics once we have docs instrumentation improvements from Elliot (docs session identifiers), and (c) Consider account-level suppression when Marketing nurture is active (separate workstream with Tyler/Camila).
